turns.en: So you competed in your first Jiu-Jitsu tournament. As a fellow Jiu-Jitsu practitioner and competitor, I think that's really inspiring given all the things you have going on. So I gotta ask, what was that experience like?
turns.zh: 所以你参加了你的第一场柔术比赛。作为同样练习和比赛柔术的人，我觉得这非常鼓舞人心，考虑到你手头有那么多事情。所以我必须问，那是什么样的体验？

turns.en: Oh, it was fun. I'm a pretty competitive person. Doing sports that basically require your full attention is really important to my mental health and the way I stay focused on everything I'm doing. I decided to get into martial arts, and it's awesome. I got a ton of my friends into it; we all train together. We have a mini Academy in my garage. One of my friends was like, 'Hey, we should go do a tournament.' I said, 'Okay, yeah, let's do it.' I'm not gonna shy away from a challenge like that. So yeah, it was awesome, just a lot of fun.
turns.zh: 哦，很有趣。我是一个很有竞争心的人。做那些需要你全神贯注的运动，对我的心理健康以及我保持专注的方式非常重要。我决定开始练武术，这太棒了。我拉了很多朋友一起练，我们都一起训练。我的车库里有一个小型道场。一个朋友说：“嘿，我们应该去参加个比赛。”我说：“好，走吧。”我不会回避这样的挑战。所以，是的，很棒，非常有趣。

turns.en: You weren't scared? There was no fear?
turns.zh: 你不害怕吗？没有恐惧？

turns.en: I don't know. I was pretty sure that I'd do okay. I like the confidence.
turns.zh: 我不知道。我很确定我会表现不错。我喜欢这种自信。

turns.en: Well, for people who don't know, Jiu-Jitsu is a martial art where you're trying to break your opponent's limbs or choke them to sleep, and do so with grace, elegance, and efficiency. It's a kind of art form that you can do for your whole life. It's basically a game, a sport of human chess. There's a lot of strategy, interesting human dynamics of using leverage. It's incredible what you can do—a small opponent can defeat a much larger opponent. But you certainly can't be distracted.
turns.zh: 嗯，对于不了解的人来说，柔术是一种试图折断对手四肢或将其勒晕的武术，而且要优雅、高效地做到。这是一种可以终身练习的艺术形式。它基本上是一种游戏，一种人类象棋的运动。有很多策略，利用杠杆的有趣人际动态。你能做到的事情令人难以置信——一个小个子对手可以击败一个大个子对手。但你绝对不能分心。

turns.en: No, it's a 100% focus sport to compete. I needed to get around the fact that I didn't want it to be this big thing. So I basically rolled up with a hat and sunglasses, wearing a COVID mask, and registered under my first and middle name—Mark Elliot. It wasn't until I pulled all that stuff off right before I got on the mat that people knew it was me. So it was pretty low-key.
turns.zh: 不，这是一项需要 100% 专注的竞技运动。我需要避免这件事闹得太大。所以我基本上戴着帽子和太阳镜，戴着口罩出现，用我的名字和中间名注册——Mark Elliot。直到我上场前摘下所有东西，人们才知道是我。所以相当低调。

turns.en: But you're still a public figure. The thing you're partially afraid of is not just losing, but being almost embarrassed. It's so raw in the sport—it's just you and another human being. There's a primal aspect there.
turns.zh: 但你仍然是公众人物。你部分害怕的不仅仅是输，而是几乎感到尴尬。这项运动如此原始——只有你和另一个人。有一种原始的一面。

turns.en: Oh yeah, it's great. For a lot of people it can be terrifying, especially the first time competing. But I think part of learning is failing. People who train Jiu-Jitsu need to not have pride. All that stuff about getting choked or joint locks—you only get into a bad situation if you're not willing to tap once you've already lost. But when you're getting started with something, you're not going to be an expert immediately, so you just need to be willing to go with that. Maybe I've just been embarrassed enough times in my life. I do think there's a thing where as people grow up, maybe they don't want to be embarrassed. They've built their adult identity and have a sense of who they are and what they want to project. To some degree, your ability to keep doing interesting things is your willingness to be embarrassed again, go back to step one, start as a beginner, get your ass kicked, and look stupid doing things. So many things we're doing—whether it's this physical part of my life or running the company—we just take on new adventures. All the big things we're doing, I think of as 10-plus-year missions. Often early on, people doubt we'll be able to do it, and the initial work seems kind of silly. Our whole ethos is we don't want to wait until something is perfect to put it out there; we want to get it out quickly and get feedback. So there's probably something about how I approach things. I just kind of think that the moment you decide you're too embarrassed to try something new, you're not going to learn anything anymore.
turns.zh: 哦，是的，这很棒。对很多人来说，这可能很可怕，尤其是第一次比赛。但我认为学习的一部分就是失败。练柔术的人不能有自尊心。所有那些关于被勒颈或关节锁的事情——如果你不愿意在已经输了的时候认输，你只会陷入糟糕的境地。但当你刚开始做某件事时，你不会立刻成为专家，所以你需要愿意接受这一点。也许我一生中已经尴尬过太多次了。我确实认为，随着人们长大，他们可能不想再尴尬了。他们建立了成年身份，对自己是谁以及想展现什么有了认知。在某种程度上，你持续做有趣事情的能力，取决于你愿意再次尴尬、回到第一步、从初学者开始、被痛扁、做事情看起来很蠢。我们做的很多事情——无论是生活中的这个身体部分，还是经营公司——我们只是接受新的冒险。我们做的所有大事，我都视为十年以上的使命。通常早期人们怀疑我们能否做到，初期工作看起来有点傻。我们的整个理念是，我们不想等到某件事完美才推出；我们想快速推出并获得反馈。所以这可能是我处理事情的方式。我只是觉得，当你决定因为太尴尬而不再尝试新事物时，你就再也学不到任何东西了。

turns.en: Like I mentioned, that fear, that anxiety could be there, could creep up every once in a while. Do you feel that in especially stressful moments outside of Jiu-Jitsu, just at work—stressful moments, big decision days, big decision moments? How do you deal with that fear, that anxiety?
turns.zh: 就像我提到的，那种恐惧、那种焦虑可能存在，偶尔会悄悄出现。在柔术之外，尤其是在工作中压力大的时刻、重大决策日、重大决策时刻，你会感受到吗？你如何处理那种恐惧和焦虑？

turns.en: The thing that stresses me out the most is always the people challenges. I kind of think that strategy questions—I tend to have enough conviction around the values of what we're trying to do and what I think matters and what I want our company to stand for—those don't really keep me up at night. It's not that I get everything right, of course I don't. We make a lot of mistakes. But I at least have a pretty strong sense of where I want us to go. The thing in running a company for almost 20 years now that's been pretty clear is when you have a team that's cohesive, you can get almost anything done. You can run through super hard challenges, make hard decisions, and push really hard to do the best work. But when there's that tension, that's when things get really tough. When I talk to other friends who run other companies, I think one of the things I actually spend a disproportionate amount of time on in running this company is just fostering a pretty tight core group of people who are running the company with me. That to me is kind of the thing that both makes it fun—having friends and people you've worked with for a while, and new people and new perspectives—but a pretty tight group who can go work on some of these things.
turns.zh: 最让我压力大的总是人员挑战。我觉得战略问题——我对自己试图做的事情的价值、我认为重要的事情以及我希望公司代表什么有足够的信念——那些并不会真的让我失眠。当然，我并不是每件事都对，我们犯了很多错误。但我至少对自己想去的方向有很强的感觉。经营公司近 20 年来，有一件事很清楚：当你有一个有凝聚力的团队时，你几乎可以完成任何事情。你可以克服超级困难的挑战，做出艰难的决定，并非常努力地推动完成最好的工作。但当存在那种紧张关系时，事情就会变得非常棘手。当我与其他经营公司的朋友交谈时，我认为我在经营这家公司上花费了不成比例的时间的一件事，就是培养一个与我一起经营公司的非常紧密的核心团队。对我来说，这既是让事情变得有趣的原因——有朋友、一起工作过的人、新人和新视角——也是一个足够紧密的团队，可以一起处理这些事情。

turns.en: Crazy things with, but to me that's also the most stressful thing is when there's tension. That weighs on me. I think it's maybe not surprising. We're a very people-focused company, and the people is the part that weighs on me the most to make sure we get right. So when there's tension in that inner circle of close folks, when you trust those folks to help you make difficult decisions about Facebook, WhatsApp, Instagram, the future of the company, the metaverse, or the AI, how do you build that close-knit group of folks to make those difficult decisions? Is there people that you have to have critical voices, very different perspectives, focusing on the past versus the future, all that kind of stuff?
turns.zh: 疯狂的事情，但对我来说，最让人压力大的就是出现紧张关系的时候。那让我很沉重。我觉得这也许并不奇怪。我们是一家非常以人为本的公司，而人是我最在意、必须确保做对的部分。所以，当你信任那些人帮你做出关于 Facebook、WhatsApp、Instagram、公司未来、元宇宙或 AI 的艰难决策时，你是如何建立那个紧密的小圈子来做出这些艰难决定的？是否需要那些持有批评声音、非常不同的视角、关注过去与未来等所有方面的人？

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, I think for one thing, it's just spending a lot of time with whatever the group is that you want to be that core group grappling with all the biggest challenges. That requires a fair amount of openness. A lot of how I run the company is, every Monday morning we get the top 30 people together. This is a group that has worked together for a long period of time. People rotate in, new people join, people leave the company or go to other roles, so it's not the same group over time. We spend a couple of hours, sometimes unstructured. I'll come with a few topics that are top of mind for me, but I'll ask other people to bring things. People raise questions: an issue happening in some country, a policy issue, a new technology developing, an issue with a partner, a design trade-off in WhatsApp between two things that are values we care about deeply. We need to decide where we want to be on that. Over time, by working through a lot of issues with people and doing it openly, people develop an intuition for each other and a bond and camaraderie. To me, developing that is a lot of the fun part of running a company or doing anything. Having people who are along on the journey that you feel like you're doing it with—nothing is ever just one person doing it.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为首先就是要花大量时间与你想让其成为核心团队的那群人在一起，共同应对所有最大的挑战。这需要相当程度的开放性。我经营公司的方式很大程度上是，每周一早上我们召集大约前 30 名高管。这是一个长期共事过的团队。人员会轮换，有新加入的，有离开公司或转到其他岗位的，所以团队并非一成不变。我们会花上几个小时，有时没有固定议程。我会提出几个我当前最关心的话题，但也会请其他人带来议题。人们会提出问题：某个国家发生的事件、政策问题、正在发展的新技术、与合作伙伴的问题、WhatsApp 中两个我们都非常看重的价值观之间的设计权衡。我们需要决定我们想站在哪一边。随着时间的推移，通过共同处理大量问题并保持开放，人们会培养出对彼此的直觉、纽带和同志情谊。对我来说，培养这种关系是经营公司或做任何事情中很有趣的部分。有那些与你同行的人，让你感觉你们是一起在做——没有任何事是单靠一个人完成的。

turns.en: Are there people that disagree often within that group?
turns.zh: 那个团队里经常有人意见不合吗？

turns.en: It's a fairly combative group.
turns.zh: 这是一个相当好辩的团队。

turns.en: Okay, so combat is part of it. So this is making decisions on design, engineering, policy, everything?
turns.zh: 好的，所以争论是其中一部分。那么这涉及设计、工程、政策等所有方面的决策吗？

turns.en: Everything, everything, everything.
turns.zh: 所有方面，所有方面，所有方面。

turns.en: I have to ask, just back to jiu-jitsu for a little bit. What's your favorite submission now that you've been doing it? How do you like to submit your opponent, Mark Zuckerberg?
turns.zh: 我不得不问一下，回到柔术一会儿。你练了这么久，最喜欢的降服技是什么？你喜欢怎么降服对手，马克·扎克伯格？

turns.en: Well, first of all, do you prefer no-gi or gi jiu-jitsu? Gi is this outfit you wear that mimics clothing so you can choke, like a kimono. It's like the traditional martial arts pajamas. Pajamas that you could choke people with. Yes, it has the lapels. So I like jiu-jitsu, I also really like MMA, and I think no-gi more closely approximates MMA. My style is maybe a little closer to an MMA style. A lot of jiu-jitsu players are fine being on their back, and having a good guard is a critical part of jiu-jitsu, but in MMA you don't want to be on your back because even if you have control, you're just taking punches while on your back. So that's no good. So you like being on top? My style is probably more pressure, and I'd probably rather be the top player. But I'm also smaller, I'm not a heavyweight guy. So from that perspective, especially because if I'm doing a competition I'll compete with people my size, but a lot of my friends are bigger than me, so back takes are probably pretty important because that's where you have the most leverage advantage. People's arms are very weak behind you. So being able to get to the back and take that is pretty important. But I don't know, I feel like the right strategy is to not be too committed to any single submission. That said, I don't like hurting people, so I always think that chokes are a somewhat more humane way to go than joint locks. It's more about control, it's less dynamic. So you're basically like a Khabib Nurmagomedov type of fighter? So let's go. Back take to a rear naked choke, I think is the clean way to go. Straightforward answer right there.
turns.zh: 首先，你喜欢无道服还是有道服柔术？道服是你穿的那种模仿衣服的装备，可以用来勒颈，就像和服。它就像传统武术的睡衣。可以用来勒人的睡衣。是的，它有翻领。所以我喜欢柔术，我也非常喜欢 MMA，我认为无道服更接近 MMA。我的风格可能更偏向 MMA 风格。很多柔术选手不介意处于下位，拥有好的防守是柔术的关键部分，但在 MMA 中你不想处于下位，因为即使你有控制，你也会在下位挨拳。所以那不好。所以你更喜欢上位？我的风格可能更偏压力型，我可能更愿意做上位选手。但我也比较小只，我不是重量级选手。所以从这个角度看，尤其因为如果参加比赛我会和同量级的人比，但我的很多朋友都比我大，所以拿背可能非常重要，因为那是你拥有最大杠杆优势的位置。人的手臂在背后非常无力。所以能够拿到背并控制住非常重要。但我不确定，我觉得正确的策略是不要太执着于任何一种降服技。话虽如此，我不喜欢伤害别人，所以我一直认为绞技比关节技更人道一些。它更注重控制，不那么动态。所以你基本上就像哈比布·努尔马戈梅多夫那种类型的 fighter？那就来吧。拿背到裸绞，我认为是最干净的方式。直接的回答。

turns.en: What advice would you give to people looking to start learning jiu-jitsu, given how busy you are, where you are in life, that you're able to do this, you're able to train, you're able to compete and get to learn something from this interesting art?
turns.zh: 鉴于你如此忙碌，处于人生的这个阶段，却还能做到这些——训练、比赛，并从这门有趣的艺术中学到东西——你会给那些想开始学习柔术的人什么建议？

turns.en: I just think you have to be willing to get beaten up a lot. Over time, I think there's a flow to all these things. One of my experiences that I think transcends running a company and the different activities I like doing is I really believe that if you're going to accomplish anything, a lot of it is just being willing to push through, having the grit and determination to push through difficult situations. For a lot of people, that ends up being a different maker between the people who get the most done and not. There are all these questions about how many days people want to work and things like that. I think almost all the people who start successful companies are working extremely hard. But one of the things you learn both by doing this over time or very acutely with things like jiu-jitsu or surfing is you can't push through everything. You learn this stuff very acutely doing sports compared to running a company, because running a company the cycle times are so long. You start a project and it's months later, or if you're building hardware it could be years later, before you're actually getting feedback and able to make the next set of decisions for the next version. Whereas one of the things that is mentally so nice about these very high turnaround conditioning activities is you get immediate feedback.
turns.zh: 我只是觉得你必须愿意经常被揍。随着时间的推移，我认为所有这些事情都有一种流动感。我的一个经历，我认为超越了经营公司和各种我喜欢做的活动，就是我坚信如果你想完成任何事情，很大程度上就是愿意坚持下去，拥有克服困难局面的毅力和决心。对很多人来说，这最终会成为那些成就最多的人与其他人之间的分水岭。关于人们想工作多少天之类的问题有很多讨论。我认为几乎所有创办成功公司的人都在极其努力地工作。但通过长期做这些事情，或者通过柔术或冲浪这类活动非常敏锐地学到的一点是，你不可能硬撑过所有事情。与经营公司相比，你通过运动非常敏锐地学到这一点，因为经营公司的周期太长。你启动一个项目，几个月后，或者如果你在构建硬件，可能几年后，你才能真正得到反馈，并为下一个版本做出下一组决策。而这些高周转率的体能训练活动在心理上非常棒的一点是，你能得到即时反馈。

ts.sec: Life Lessons from Sports and Jiu-Jitsu

turns.en: Sports things like that, you get feedback very quickly. It's like, okay, I don't counter something correctly, you get punched in the face. Not in Jiu-Jitsu, you don't get punched, but in MMA. There are all these analogies between these things that I think actually hold, that are important life lessons. It's like, you're surfing a wave. Sometimes you can't go in the other direction. There are limits. With foil, you can pump and push hard in a bunch of directions, but at some level, the momentum against you is strong enough that it's not going to work. I do think that's a humbling but important lesson for people who are running things or building things. A lot of the game is just being able to push and work through complicated things, but you also need enough understanding of which things you just can't push through and where the finesse is more important.
turns.zh: 像运动这类事情，反馈来得非常快。比如，我没正确格挡，就会挨一拳。柔术里不会挨拳，但综合格斗会。这些事之间有很多类比，我觉得它们确实是重要的人生道理。就像冲浪，有时候你没法往反方向走，总有限制。用水翼板，你可以泵动并在很多方向上用力推，但在某个层面上，逆势的动量太强，就是行不通。我认为这对经营或建设的人来说，是一个既让人谦卑又很重要的教训。很多时候，游戏的关键就是能推动并解决复杂问题，但你也需要足够理解哪些事是推不动的，以及哪里更需要巧劲。

turns.en: Yeah. What are your Jiu-Jitsu life lessons?
turns.zh: 对。你的柔术人生道理是什么？

turns.en: Well, I think you did it. You made it sound so simple and eloquent that it's easy to miss. But basically, being okay and accepting the wisdom and the joy in getting your ass kicked, in the full range of what that means. I think that's a big gift of being humbled. Somehow being humbled, especially physically, opens your mind to the full process of learning what it means to learn, which is being willing to suck at something. Jiu-Jitsu very repetitively and efficiently humbles you over and over and over, to where you can carry that lesson to places where you don't get humbled as much, whether it's research or running a company or building stuff. The cycle is longer. You can get humbled in a period of an hour over and over, especially when you're a beginner. A little person, somebody much smaller than you, just kicks your ass repeatedly, definitively, where there's no argument. And then you literally tap, because if you don't tap, you're going to die. So this is an agreement: you could have killed me just now, but we're friends, so we agree you're not going to. That kind of humbling process does something to your psyche, to your ego, that puts it in its proper context. It makes you realize that everything in this life is like a journey from sucking through a hard process of improving rigorously day after day after day. Any kind of success requires hard work. Jiu-Jitsu, more than a lot of sports I've done, really teaches you that. And you made it sound so simple: it's okay, it's part of the process. You just get humble. I've just failed and been embarrassed so many times in my life that it's a core competence at this point.
turns.zh: 嗯，我觉得你做到了。你说得那么简单又富有感染力，以至于容易让人忽略。但本质上，就是坦然接受被痛揍的智慧和乐趣，接受它全部的含义。我认为这是谦卑带来的大礼物。不知怎的，谦卑——尤其是身体上的谦卑——会让你敞开心扉，去真正理解学习的全过程，也就是愿意在某件事上做得一塌糊涂。柔术会非常重复且高效地让你一次次谦卑，直到你能把那个教训带到那些不那么容易受挫的地方，无论是研究、经营公司还是做产品。那个周期更长。你可以在一小时内反复受挫，尤其是初学者。一个小个子，比你小得多的人，反复、明确地揍你，毫无争议。然后你真的拍地认输，因为不拍你就会死。所以这是一种约定：你刚才本可以杀了我，但我们是朋友，所以我们约定你不会那么做。那种谦卑的过程会对你的心理和自负产生影响，把它放到合适的位置。它让你意识到，这辈子的一切都像一场从糟糕到通过艰苦过程日复一日严格改进的旅程。任何成功都需要努力。柔术比我做过的很多运动都更能教会你这一点。而你把它说得那么简单：没事，这是过程的一部分。你只要谦卑。我这辈子失败和尴尬的次数太多了，以至于这现在成了我的核心能力。

turns.en: Well, yes, and there's a deep truth to that. Being able to, and you said it in the very beginning, that's the thing that stops us, especially as you get older, especially as you develop expertise in certain areas: the not being willing to be a beginner in a new area. Because that's where the growth happens, is being willing to be a beginner, being willing to be embarrassed, saying something stupid, doing something stupid. A lot of us that get good at one thing want to show that off, and it sucks being a beginner, but it's where growth happens.
turns.zh: 嗯，是的，这其中有深刻的道理。能够——而且你一开始就说了——这正是阻碍我们的东西，尤其是随着年龄增长，当你在某些领域成为专家时：不愿意在新领域当新手。因为成长就发生在那里，愿意当新手，愿意出丑，说傻话，做傻事。我们很多人在一件事上做得好，就想炫耀，当新手很糟糕，但那是成长发生的地方。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

ts.sec: Meta's AI Development and Llama Model

turns.en: Well, speaking of which, let me ask you about AI. It seems like this year, for the entirety of human civilization, is an interesting year for the development of artificial intelligence. A lot of interesting stuff is happening. Meta is a big part of that. Meta has developed Llama, which is a 65 billion parameter model. There are a lot of interesting questions to ask here, one of which has to do with open source. But first, can you tell the story of developing this model and making the complicated decision of how to release it?
turns.zh: 说到这个，让我问问你关于 AI 的事。看起来今年，对于整个人类文明来说，是人工智能发展中有趣的一年。很多有趣的事情正在发生。Meta 是其中的重要部分。Meta 开发了 Llama，一个 650 亿参数的模型。这里有很多有趣的问题可以问，其中一个与开源有关。但首先，你能讲讲开发这个模型以及做出如何发布它的复杂决定的故事吗？

turns.en: Yeah, sure. I think you're right. First of all, in the last year, there have been a bunch of advances on scaling up these large Transformer models. There's the language equivalent with large language models, and the image generation equivalent with these large diffusion models. There's a lot of fundamental research that's gone into this. Meta has taken the approach of being quite open and academic in our development of AI. Part of this is we want to have the best people in the world researching this, and a lot of the best people want to know that they're going to be able to share their work. So that's part of the deal we have: if you're one of the top AI researchers in the world, you can come here, get access to industry-scale infrastructure, and part of our ethos is that we want to share what's invented broadly. We do that with a lot of the different AI tools we create. Llama is the language model that our research team made. We did a limited open source release for it, intended for researchers to be able to use it. But the responsibility and getting safety right on these is very important. So we didn't think that for the first one, there were a bunch of questions around whether we should be releasing this commercially. We kind of punted on that for V1 of Llama and just released it for research. Now, obviously, by releasing it for research, it's out there, but companies know that they're not supposed to put it into commercial releases. We're working on the follow-up models and thinking through how exactly this should work for follow-ons, now that we've had time to work on a lot more of the safety and the pieces around that. But overall, I just kind of think that it would be good if there were a lot of different folks who had the ability to build state-of-the-art technology here, not just a small number of big companies. To train one of these state-of-the-art AI models takes hundreds of millions of dollars of infrastructure. So there are not that many organizations in the world that can do that at the biggest scale today. It gets more efficient every day, so I do think that will be available to more folks over time. But I just think there's all this innovation out there that people can create.
turns.zh: 嗯，当然。首先，你说得对。在过去一年里，在扩展这些大型 Transformer 模型方面取得了一系列进展。有大型语言模型的语言版本，也有大型扩散模型的图像生成版本。这背后有很多基础研究。Meta 在 AI 开发中采取了相当开放和学术化的方式。部分原因是，我们希望世界上最优秀的人来研究这个，而很多最优秀的人希望知道他们能够分享自己的工作。所以这是我们协议的一部分：如果你是世界上最顶尖的 AI 研究者之一，你可以来这里，获得行业规模的算力基础设施，而我们的理念之一就是广泛分享发明。我们用很多不同的 AI 工具都这样做。Llama 是我们的研究团队制作的语言模型。我们做了一个有限的开源发布，旨在让研究人员能够使用它。但责任和确保这些模型的安全性非常重要。所以对于第一个版本，我们觉得有很多关于是否应该商业发布的问题。我们在 Llama V1 上回避了这个问题，只做了研究发布。现在，显然，通过研究发布，它已经公开了，但公司知道他们不应该将其用于商业发布。我们正在开发后续模型，并思考后续版本到底应该如何运作，因为我们现在有更多时间来处理安全性和相关方面。但总的来说，我只是认为，如果有更多不同的人有能力在这里构建最先进的技术，而不是只有少数大公司，那会很好。训练这些最先进的 AI 模型需要数亿美元的算力基础设施。所以当今世界上没有多少组织能在最大规模上做到这一点。它每天都在变得更高效，所以我认为随着时间的推移，它会对更多人可用。但我只是觉得，人们可以创造所有这些创新。

ts.sec: Open Source Community Response to LLaMA

turns.en: For people who don't know, you did the limited release in February of this year of LLaMA, and it got quote-unquote leaked, meaning it escaped the limited release aspect. But it was something you probably anticipated, given that it's just released to researchers. We shared it with researchers, right? So it's just trying to make sure that there's like a slow release. But from there, I would love to get your comment on what happened next, which is this very vibrant open source community that just builds stuff on top of it. There's LLaMA CPP, basically stuff that makes it more efficient to run on smaller computers. There's combining with reinforcement learning with human feedback, so some of the different interesting fine-tuning mechanisms. There's also fine-tuning and GPT-3 generations. There's a lot of GPT-4 All, Alpaca, Colossal AI, all these kinds of models that just spring up and run on top of it. What do you think about that?
turns.zh: 对于不了解的人来说，你在今年 2 月有限发布了 LLaMA，它被“泄露”了，意思是它脱离了有限发布的范围。但考虑到你只是把它发布给研究人员，这可能是你预料之中的事。我们把它分享给了研究人员，对吧？所以只是想确保有一个缓慢的发布过程。但我想听听你对接下来发生的事情的看法——一个非常活跃的开源社区在它的基础上构建了很多东西。有 LLaMA CPP，基本上让它在更小的计算机上运行得更高效。有结合基于人类反馈的强化学习（RLHF）的各种有趣的微调机制。还有微调和 GPT-3 生成。有很多 GPT-4 All、Alpaca、Colossal AI 这类模型，它们突然出现并运行在 LLaMA 之上。你怎么看？

turns.en: I think it's been really neat to see. There have been folks who are getting it to run on local devices. If you're an individual who just wants to experiment at home, you probably don't have a large budget to get access to a lot of cloud compute, so getting it to run on your local laptop is pretty good and relevant. Then there are things like LLaMA CPP that reimplemented it more efficiently. Now even when we run our own versions of it, we can do it on way less compute, it's way more efficient, and saves a lot of money for everyone who uses this. That is good. I do think it's worth calling out that because this was a relatively early release, LLaMA isn't quite on the frontier as, for example, the biggest OpenAI models or the biggest Google models. The largest LLaMA model we released had 65 billion parameters, and no one outside of OpenAI knows exactly what the specs are for GPT-4, but my understanding is it's like 10 times bigger. I think Google's PaLM model also has about 10 times as many parameters. Now the LLaMA models are very efficient, so they perform well for something that's around 65 billion parameters. For me, that was also part of this because there's this whole debate around whether it's good for everyone in the world to have access to the most frontier AI models. As the models start approaching something like superhuman intelligence, that's a bigger question we'll have to grapple with. But right now, these are still very basic tools. They're powerful in the sense that a lot of open source software like databases or web servers can enable a lot of pretty important things, but I don't think anyone looks at the current generation of LLaMA and thinks it's anywhere near a superintelligence. So I think a bunch of those questions around whether it's good to get it out there — at this stage, surely you want more researchers working on it for all the reasons that open source software has a lot of advantages. We talked about efficiency before, but another one is that open source software tends to be more secure because you have more people looking at it openly and scrutinizing it, finding holes in it, and that makes it more safe. So I think at this point, it's generally agreed upon that open source software is generally more secure and safer than things that are developed in a silo where people try to get security through obscurity. So for the scale of what we're seeing now with AI, I think we're more likely to get to good alignment and good understanding of what needs to be done to make this work well by having it be open source. That's something that is quite good to have out there and happening publicly at this point.
turns.zh: 我觉得这真的很棒。有人已经让它能在本地设备上运行了。如果你只是个人想在家实验，可能没有太多预算去获取大量云计算资源，所以能在本地笔记本上运行就很好，也很实用。还有像 LLaMA CPP 这样的东西，它更高效地重新实现了模型。现在即使我们自己运行自己的版本，也可以用更少的算力，效率高得多，为每个使用它的人省了很多钱。这很好。但我觉得值得指出的是，因为这是一个相对早期的发布，LLaMA 并不像最大的 OpenAI 模型或最大的 Google 模型那样处于前沿。我们发布的最大 LLaMA 模型有 650 亿参数，除了 OpenAI 内部没人知道 GPT-4 的具体规格，但据我所知它大约大 10 倍。我认为 Google 的 PaLM 模型也有大约 10 倍的参数。不过 LLaMA 模型非常高效，所以对于 650 亿参数左右的规模来说，它们表现很好。对我来说，这也是问题的一部分，因为有一个争论：让全世界所有人都能访问最前沿的 AI 模型是否好。当模型开始接近超级智能时，那将是一个我们必须应对的更大问题。但现在，这些仍然是相当基础的工具。它们很强大，就像很多开源软件（如数据库或 Web 服务器）能实现很多重要的事情一样，但我不认为有人会看着当前这一代 LLaMA 觉得它接近超级智能。所以我认为那些关于是否应该把它放出去的问题——在这个阶段，你当然希望更多的研究人员参与进来，因为开源软件有很多优势。我们之前谈到了效率，但另一个优势是开源软件往往更安全，因为更多的人公开审视它，发现漏洞，这使它更安全。所以我认为在这一点上，人们普遍同意开源软件通常比闭门开发、试图通过隐蔽实现安全的东西更安全。因此，对于我们现在看到的 AI 规模，我认为通过开源，我们更有可能实现良好的对齐和对如何让它工作良好的理解。这是目前公开进行的好事。

ts.sec: Meta's Open Source Strategy and Future

turns.en: Meta released a lot of models as open source, so the Mass Multilingual Speech Model, the Age Model. I'll ask you questions about those, but the point is you've open sourced quite a lot, you've been spearheading the open source movement. That's really positive and inspiring to see from one angle. From the research angle, of course there are folks who are really terrified about the existential threat of artificial intelligence, and those folks will say that you have to be careful about the open sourcing step. But where do you see the future of open source here as part of Meta? The tension here is: do you want to release the magic sauce? That's one tension. And the other one is: do you want to put a powerful tool in the hands of bad actors, even though it probably has a huge amount of positive impact also?
turns.zh: Meta 开源了很多模型，比如 Mass 多语言语音模型、Age 模型。我会问你关于这些的问题，但重点是你们开源了很多，你一直在引领开源运动。从一个角度看，这非常积极和鼓舞人心。从研究角度看，当然有人对人工智能的生存威胁感到非常恐惧，他们会说你在开源步骤上必须小心。但作为 Meta 的一部分，你如何看待开源的未来？这里有一个张力：你想不想释放魔法配方？这是一个张力。另一个是：你想不想把强大的工具交到坏人手中，尽管它也可能带来巨大的积极影响？

turns.en: Again, I think for the stage that we're at in the development of AI, I don't think anyone looks at the current state of things and thinks that this is superintelligence. The models we're talking about, the LLaMA models here, are generally an order of magnitude smaller than what OpenAI or Google are doing. So I think that at least for the stage we're at now, the equities balance strongly in my view towards doing this more openly. I think if you got something that was closer to superintelligence, then you'd have to discuss that more and think through that a lot more. We haven't made a decision yet as to what we would do if we were in that position, but I think there's a good chance that we're pretty far off from that position. So I'm certainly not saying that the position we're taking on this now applies to every single thing we would ever do. Certainly inside the company, we probably do more open source work than most other big tech companies, but we also don't open source everything. A lot of our core...
turns.zh: 再次强调，我认为在 AI 发展的当前阶段，没有人会认为现状是超级智能。我们讨论的模型，比如 LLaMA 模型，通常比 OpenAI 或 Google 正在做的模型小一个数量级。所以我认为，至少在我们现在的阶段，天平强烈倾向于更开放地做这件事。我认为如果出现了更接近超级智能的东西，那么你就需要更多地讨论和思考这个问题。我们还没有决定如果处于那种情况会怎么做，但我认为我们离那个位置还很远。所以我当然不是说我们现在采取的立场适用于我们做的每一件事。在公司内部，我们可能比大多数其他大型科技公司做了更多的开源工作，但我们也没有开源所有东西。很多核心……

turns.en: Of app code for WhatsApp or Instagram or something, I mean, we're not open sourcing that. It's not like a general enough piece of software that would be useful for a lot of people to do different things. Whereas the software that we do, whether it's like an open source server design or basically things like McRouter, which was probably our earliest project that I worked on, it was probably one of the last things that I coded and led directly for the company. But basically this caching tool for quick data retrieval, these are things that are just broadly useful across anything that you want to build. And I think that some of the language models now have that feel as well, as some of the other things that we're building like the translation tool that you just referenced.
turns.zh: 像 WhatsApp 或 Instagram 的应用代码，我们不会开源。它不是那种通用到能让很多人做不同事情的软件。而我们开源的软件，比如开源服务器设计，或者像 McRouter 这样的东西——那可能是我在公司最早参与的项目，也是我最后亲自编码和主导的项目之一——这个缓存工具用于快速数据检索，这些是构建任何东西都广泛有用的基础组件。我认为现在的一些语言模型也有这种感觉，还有我们正在构建的其他东西，比如你刚才提到的翻译工具。

turns.en: So text-to-speech and speech-to-text, you've expanded it from around 100 languages to more than 1,000 languages, and you can identify more than the model can identify more than 4,000 spoken languages, which is 40 times more than any known previous technology. To me, that's really, really, really exciting in terms of connecting the world, breaking down barriers that language creates.
turns.zh: 所以文本转语音和语音转文本，你们从大约 100 种语言扩展到了超过 1,000 种语言，而且模型能识别超过 4,000 种口语，这是任何已知先前技术的 40 倍。对我来说，这在连接世界、打破语言障碍方面真的非常非常令人兴奋。

turns.en: Yeah, I think being able to translate between all of these different pieces in real time, this has been a kind of common sci-fi idea that we'd all have, whether it's an earbud or glasses or something that can help translate in real time between all these different languages. And that's one that I think technology is basically delivering now. So I think yeah, I think that's pretty exciting.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为能够实时翻译所有这些不同语言，这一直是一个常见的科幻想法——无论是耳机、眼镜还是其他东西，能帮助实时翻译不同语言。我认为技术现在基本上正在实现这一点。所以我觉得这非常令人兴奋。

turns.en: You mentioned the next version of Llama. What can you say about the next version of Llama? What were you working on in terms of release, in terms of the vision for that?
turns.zh: 你提到了 Llama 的下一个版本。关于下一个版本的 Llama，你能说些什么？在发布方面，在愿景方面，你们在做什么？

turns.en: Well, a lot of what we're doing is taking the first version, which was primarily this research version, and trying to now build a version that has all of the latest state-of-the-art safety precautions built in. And we're using some more data to train it from across our services. But a lot of the work that we're doing internally is really just focused on making sure that this is as aligned and responsible as possible. And we're building a lot of our own product experiences to help people connect and express themselves. So you'll have an assistant that you can talk to in WhatsApp. I think in the future, every creator will have kind of an AI agent that can act on their behalf that their fans can talk to. I want to get to the point where every small business basically has an AI agent that people can talk to for commerce and customer support and things like that. So there are going to be all these different things, and Llama or the language model underlying this is basically going to be the engine that powers that. The reason to open source it is that, as we did with the first version, it basically unlocks a lot of innovation in the ecosystem. We will make our products better as well, and also gives us a lot of valuable feedback on security and safety, which is important for making this good. But yeah, the work that we're doing to advance the infrastructure is basically taking it beyond a research project into something which is ready to be core infrastructure, not only for our own products but hopefully for a lot of other things out there too.
turns.zh: 我们正在做的主要工作是，以第一个版本（主要是研究版本）为基础，尝试构建一个内置了所有最新最先进安全措施的版本。我们正在使用来自我们服务的更多数据来训练它。但我们内部的大量工作实际上只是专注于确保它尽可能对齐和负责任。我们也在构建许多自己的产品体验，帮助人们连接和表达自己。所以你会有一个可以在 WhatsApp 中对话的助手。我认为未来每个创作者都会拥有一个 AI 智能体，代表他们行动，让粉丝可以与之交谈。我希望达到每个小企业都有一个 AI 智能体的程度，人们可以与之交谈进行商务和客户支持等。所以会有所有这些不同的东西，而 Llama 或底层的语言模型基本上将成为驱动这一切的引擎。开源的原因是，正如我们在第一个版本中所做的那样，它基本上释放了生态系统中的大量创新。我们也会让自己的产品变得更好，并且还能获得大量关于安全性的宝贵反馈，这对做好这件事很重要。但我们推进基础设施的工作基本上是在把它从一个研究项目变成准备成为核心基础设施的东西，不仅用于我们自己的产品，也希望用于其他许多地方。

turns.en: Do you think the Llama or the language model underlying that version too will be open sourced? Do you have internal debate around that, the pros and cons and so on?
turns.zh: 你认为 Llama 或那个版本底层的语言模型也会开源吗？你们内部对此有辩论吗，关于利弊等等？

turns.en: This is, I mean, we were talking about the debates that we have internally. And I think the question is how to do it right. I mean, we did the research license for V1. And I think the big thing that we're thinking about is basically what's the right way. So there was a leak that happened. I don't know if you can comment on it. For V1, we released it as a research project for researchers to be able to use. But in doing so, we put it out there. We were very clear that anyone who uses the code and the weights doesn't have a commercial license to put into products. And we've generally seen people respect that right. There aren't any reputable companies that are basically trying to put this into their commercial products. But by sharing it with so many researchers, it did leave the building. But what have you learned from that process that you might be able to apply to V2 about how to release it safely and effectively?
turns.zh: 我的意思是，我们一直在讨论内部的辩论。我认为问题是如何正确地做。我们为 V1 做了研究许可证。我认为我们正在考虑的大事基本上是什么是正确的做法。所以有一次泄露事件。我不知道你是否能评论。对于 V1，我们将其作为研究项目发布，供研究人员使用。但这样做，我们把它放出去了。我们非常明确地表示，任何使用代码和权重的人都没有商业许可以将其用于产品。我们通常看到人们尊重这一点。没有任何知名公司试图将其用于商业产品。但通过与这么多研究人员分享，它确实离开了公司。但你从那个过程中学到了什么，可以应用到 V2 上，关于如何安全有效地发布它？

turns.en: If you release it, yeah.
turns.zh: 如果你发布它，是的。

turns.en: Well, I mean, I think a lot of the feedback, like I said, is just around different things around how do you fine-tune models to make them more aligned and safer. And you see all the different data recipes that you mentioned a lot of different projects that are based on this. There's one at Berkeley, there are just all over. And people have tried a lot of different things, and we've tried a bunch of stuff internally. So we're making progress here, but also we're able to learn from some of the best ideas in the community. And I think we want to just continue pushing that forward. But I don't have any news to announce on this, if that's what you're asking. I mean, this is a thing that we're still actively working through the right way to move forward here. The details of the secret sauce are still being developed.
turns.zh: 嗯，我的意思是，很多反馈，就像我说的，只是围绕如何微调模型使其更对齐和更安全的不同方面。你看到了所有不同的数据配方，你提到了很多基于此的项目。伯克利有一个，到处都是。人们尝试了很多不同的东西，我们内部也尝试了很多。所以我们正在取得进展，同时也能从社区中一些最好的想法中学习。我认为我们想继续推动这一点。但我没有任何消息要宣布，如果这是你问的。我的意思是，我们仍在积极研究如何正确推进。秘密配方的细节仍在开发中。

turns.en: I see. You comment on what do you think of the thing that worked for GPT, which is the reinforcement learning with human feedback, so doing this alignment process. Do you find it interesting? And as part of that, let me ask because I talked to Yann LeCun before talking to you today. He asked me to ask, or suggested that I ask: do you think LLM fine-tuning will need to be crowdsourced Wikipedia style? So crowd sourcing, this kind of idea of how to integrate the human in the fine-tuning of these foundation models.
turns.zh: 我明白了。你评论一下你对 GPT 有效的方法的看法，也就是基于人类反馈的强化学习（RLHF），即进行对齐过程。你觉得它有趣吗？作为其中的一部分，让我问一下，因为今天和你谈话之前我和 Yann LeCun 聊过。他让我问，或者建议我问：你认为 LLM 微调是否需要像维基百科那样众包？所以众包，这种将人类整合到这些基础模型微调中的想法。

turns.en: Yeah, I think that's a really interesting idea that I've talked to Yann about a bunch. And we were talking about how do you basically train these models to be as safe and aligned and responsible as possible. And different groups out there who do development test different data recipes and fine-tuning.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为这是一个非常有趣的想法，我和 Yann 讨论过很多次。我们在讨论如何训练这些模型使其尽可能安全、对齐和负责任。不同的开发团队测试不同的数据配方和微调方法。

turns.en: This idea that you just mentioned is that at the end of the day, instead of having one group fine-tune some stuff and then another group produce a different fine-tuning recipe, and then us trying to figure out which one we think works best to produce the most aligned model, I do think that it would be nice if you could get to a point where you had a Wikipedia-style collaborative way for a broader community to fine-tune it as well. Now, there's a lot of challenges in that, both from an infrastructure, community management, and product perspective about how you do that. So I haven't worked that out yet, but as an idea, I think it's quite compelling, and I think it goes well with the ethos of open-sourcing the technology, also finding a way to have a community-driven training of it. But I think that there are a lot of questions on this in general. These questions around what's the best way to produce aligned AI models, it's very much a research area, and it's one that I think we will need to make as much progress on as the kind of core intelligence capability of the models themselves.
turns.zh: 你刚才提到的这个想法是，归根结底，与其让一个小组微调一些东西，然后另一个小组提出不同的微调方案，再由我们试图找出哪个方案能产生最对齐的模型，我确实认为，如果能达到一个类似维基百科的协作方式，让更广泛的社区也能参与微调，那会很好。当然，这有很多挑战，无论是从基础设施、社区管理还是产品角度来看，如何实现这一点。我还没有完全想清楚，但作为一个想法，我觉得它很有吸引力，而且它与开源技术的精神很契合，也找到了一种社区驱动训练的方式。但我认为，总的来说，这方面还有很多问题。这些关于如何产生对齐的 AI 模型的问题，本质上是一个研究领域，我认为我们需要在这个领域取得与模型核心智能能力本身同样多的进展。

turns.en: Well, I just did a conversation with Jimmy Wales, the founder of Wikipedia, and to me, Wikipedia is one of the greatest websites ever created and is a kind of miracle that it works. And I think it has to do with something that you mentioned, which is community. You have a small community of editors that somehow work together well, and they handle very controversial topics with balance and grace, despite the attacks that will often happen. A lot of the time, I mean, it has issues just like any other human system, but yes, the balance is amazing what they've been able to achieve. But it's also not perfect, and I think that there are still a lot of challenges. The more controversial the topic, the more difficult the journey towards truth or knowledge or wisdom that Wikipedia aspires to capture. In the same way, AI models will need to be able to generate those same things: truth, knowledge, and wisdom. And how do you align those models so that they generate something that is closest to truth? There are these concerns about misinformation, all this kind of stuff that nobody can define. And it's something that we together as a human species have to define: what is truth, and how to help AI systems generate that. One of the things language models do really well is generate convincing-sounding things that can be completely wrong. So how do you align it to be less wrong? Part of that is the training, and part of that is the alignment, however you do the alignment stage. And just like you said, it's a very new and very open research problem.
turns.zh: 嗯，我刚刚和维基百科的创始人 Jimmy Wales 聊过。对我来说，维基百科是有史以来最伟大的网站之一，它能够运作简直是个奇迹。我认为这与你提到的社区有关。有一小群编辑以某种方式很好地合作，他们处理非常有争议的话题时，尽管经常受到攻击，却能保持平衡和优雅。很多时候，它也有问题，就像任何其他人类系统一样，但他们的成就是惊人的。但它也并不完美，我认为仍然有很多挑战。话题越有争议，维基百科试图捕捉真理、知识或智慧的旅程就越困难。同样，AI 模型也需要能够生成同样的东西：真理、知识和智慧。那么，如何对齐这些模型，使它们生成最接近真理的东西呢？存在这些关于错误信息的担忧，所有这类没人能定义的东西。这是我们作为人类物种必须共同定义的东西：什么是真理，以及如何帮助 AI 系统生成真理。语言模型非常擅长的一件事就是生成听起来很有说服力但可能完全错误的东西。那么，如何对齐它，让它不那么错误呢？部分在于训练，部分在于对齐，无论你如何做对齐阶段。正如你所说，这是一个非常新且非常开放的研究问题。

turns.en: Yeah, and I think that there's also a lot of questions about whether the current architecture for LLMs, as you continue scaling it, what happens. I mean, a lot of what's been exciting in the last year is that there was clearly a qualitative breakthrough where, with some of the GPT models that OpenAI put out and that others have been able to do as well, I think it reached a kind of level of quality where people like, wow, this feels different, and it's going to be able to be the foundation for building a lot of awesome products and experiences and value. But I think the other realization that people have is, wow, we just made a breakthrough. If there are other breakthroughs quickly, then I think that there's the sense that maybe we're closer to general intelligence. But I think that idea is predicated on the idea that people believe there's still generally a bunch of additional breakthroughs to make, and we just don't know how long it's going to take to get there. One view that some people have, this doesn't tend to be my view as much, is that simply scaling the current LLMs and getting to higher parameter count models by itself will get us to something that is closer to general intelligence. But I don't know, I tend to think that there are probably more fundamental steps that need to be taken along the way. But still, the leap taken with this extra alignment step is quite incredible, quite surprising to a lot of folks. And on top of that, when you start to have hundreds of millions of people potentially using a product that integrates that, you can start to see civilization-transforming effects before you achieve superintelligence. It could be super transformative without being a superintelligence.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为还有很多问题关于当前的 LLM 架构，当你继续扩展它时会发生什么。我的意思是，过去一年令人兴奋的很多事情是，显然出现了一个质的突破，OpenAI 发布的一些 GPT 模型以及其他公司也能做到类似的事情，我认为它达到了一种质量水平，让人们觉得，哇，这感觉不一样了，它将成为构建许多优秀产品、体验和价值的基础。但我认为人们另一个认识是，哇，我们刚刚取得了一个突破。如果很快还有其他突破，那么我觉得可能我们更接近通用智能了。但我认为这个想法是建立在人们相信仍然需要一系列额外突破的基础上的，而我们只是不知道需要多长时间才能达到那里。有些人持有的一种观点——这不太是我的观点——是仅仅通过扩展当前的 LLM 并达到更高的参数量，我们就能得到更接近通用智能的东西。但我不确定，我倾向于认为沿途可能还需要更多根本性的步骤。不过，通过这个额外的对齐步骤所取得的飞跃，对很多人来说是非常不可思议、非常令人惊讶的。除此之外，当你有数亿人可能使用集成了它的产品时，你可以在实现超级智能之前就看到改变文明的效果。它可以在不是超级智能的情况下就具有超级变革性。

turns.en: Oh yeah. I mean, I think that there are going to be a lot of amazing products and value that can be created with the current level of technology. To some degree, I'm excited to work on a lot of those products over the next few years, and I think it would just create a tremendous amount of whiplash if the number of breakthroughs keeps stacking, because I think to some degree industry and the world needs some time to build these breakthroughs into the products and experiences that we all use so we can actually benefit from them. But I don't know, I think that there's just an awesome amount of stuff to do. I think about all of the small businesses or individual entrepreneurs out there who now are going to be able to get help coding the things that they need to go build things, or designing the things that they need, or will be able to use these models to do customer support for the people that they're serving over WhatsApp without having to... I think that's just going to be super exciting. It's going to create better experiences for people and just unlock a ton of innovation and value. So, I don't know if you know, but over three billion people use WhatsApp, Facebook, and Instagram. So any kind of AI-fueled products that go into that, we're talking about anything with LLMs, will have a tremendous amount of impact. Do you have ideas and thoughts about possible products that might start being integrated into these platforms used by so many people?
turns.zh: 哦，是的。我的意思是，我认为用当前的技术水平可以创造出很多令人惊叹的产品和价值。在某种程度上，我很兴奋能在未来几年致力于这些产品，我认为如果突破不断叠加，那将造成巨大的冲击，因为我认为在某种程度上，行业和世界需要一些时间将这些突破融入到我们所有人使用的产品和体验中，这样我们才能真正从中受益。但我不确定，我认为有太多事情要做。我想到了所有的小企业或个人创业者，他们现在将能够获得帮助来编写他们需要构建的东西的代码，或者设计他们需要的东西，或者能够使用这些模型来为他们在 WhatsApp 上服务的人提供客户支持，而不必……我认为那将非常令人兴奋。它将为人们创造更好的体验，并释放大量的创新和价值。所以，我不知道你是否知道，但有超过 30 亿人使用 WhatsApp、Facebook 和 Instagram。因此，任何融入其中的 AI 驱动产品——我们说的是任何涉及 LLM 的东西——都将产生巨大的影响。你对可能开始集成到这些被如此多人使用的平台上的产品有什么想法和看法吗？

turns.en: Yeah, I think there are three main categories of things that we're working on. The first, that I think is probably the most interesting, is there's this notion of you're going to have an assistant or an agent who you can talk to. And I think probably the biggest thing that's different about my view of how this plays out from what I see with OpenAI and Google and others is, everyone else is building like the one singular AI.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为我们正在研究的主要有三类东西。第一个，我认为可能是最有趣的，就是你会有一个可以与之交谈的助手或智能体。我认为我的观点与 OpenAI、Google 等公司的观点最大的不同在于，其他人都在构建一个单一的 AI。

ts.sec: Multiple AIs vs. one singular AI

turns.en: Right, it's like okay you talk to ChatGPT or you talk to Bard or you talk to Bing. And my view is that there are going to be a lot of different AIs that people are going to want to engage with, just like you want to use a number of different apps for different things and you have relationships with different people in your life who fill different emotional roles for you. So I think that people have a reason that I think you don't just want a singular AI. And that I think is probably the biggest distinction in terms of how I think about this. A bunch of these things: I think you'll want an assistant. I mentioned a couple of these before. I think every creator who you interact with will ultimately want some kind of AI that can proxy them and be something that their fans can interact with, or that allows them to interact with their fans. This is like the common creator prize: everyone's trying to build a community and engage with people, and they want tools to be able to amplify themselves more and be able to do that. But you only have 24 hours in a day, so I think having the ability to basically bottle up your personality, or give your fans information about when you're performing a concert or something like that, is going to be something that's super valuable. But it's not just that. Again, it's not this idea that I think people are going to want just one singular AI. I think you're going to want to interact with a lot of different entities. And then I think there's the business version of this too, which we've touched on a couple of times, which is: I think every business in the world is going to want basically an AI that, you know, it's like you have your page on Instagram or Facebook or WhatsApp or whatever, and you want to point people to an AI that people can interact with, but you want to know that that AI is only going to sell your products. You don't want it recommending your competitor stuff, right? So it's not like there can be just one singular AI that can answer all the questions for a person, because that AI might not actually be aligned with you as a business to really do the best job providing support for your product. So I think that there's going to be a clear need in the market and in people's lives for there to be a bunch of these.
turns.zh: 没错，就像你跟 ChatGPT 聊，或者跟 Bard 聊，或者跟 Bing 聊。我的看法是，人们会想跟很多不同的 AI 互动，就像你会用很多不同的 App 做不同的事，你生活中也有不同的人扮演不同的情感角色。所以我认为人们有理由不想要单一的 AI。这大概是我思考这个问题时最大的区别。其中很多事情：我觉得你会想要一个助手。我之前提过几个。我认为你接触的每个创作者最终都会想要某种 AI 来代理他们，让他们的粉丝可以互动，或者让他们与粉丝互动。这是创作者的共同目标：每个人都在努力建立社区并与人们互动，他们想要工具来放大自己并做到这一点。但你一天只有 24 小时，所以我认为能够基本上封装你的个性，或者向粉丝提供你何时举办音乐会之类的信息，将是非常有价值的。但不仅仅是这些。再说一次，这不是我认为人们只想要一个单一 AI 的想法。我认为你会想与许多不同的实体互动。然后还有商业版本，我们之前也提到过几次：我认为世界上每个企业都会想要一个 AI，就像你在 Instagram、Facebook 或 WhatsApp 上有主页一样，你想把人们引向一个可以互动的 AI，但你要知道那个 AI 只会销售你的产品。你不想让它推荐竞争对手的东西，对吧？所以不可能只有一个单一的 AI 能回答一个人的所有问题，因为那个 AI 可能实际上并不与你作为企业保持一致，无法真正为你的产品提供最好的支持。所以我认为市场和人们的生活中显然需要很多这样的 AI。

turns.en: Part of that is figuring out the research, the technology that enables the personalization that you're talking about. So not one centralized godlike LLM, but just a huge diversity of them that's fine-tuned to particular needs, particular styles, particular businesses, particular brands, all that kind of stuff. And also enabling people to create them really easily for your own business or if you're a creator, to be able to help you engage with your fans. And I think that's a clear kind of interesting product direction here that I think is fairly unique from what you, I, any of the other big companies are taking. It also aligns well with this sort of open source approach, because again we sort of believe in this more community-oriented, more democratic approach to building out the products and technology around this. We don't think that there's going to be the one true thing; we think that there should be kind of a lot of development. So that part of things I think is going to be really interesting, and we could spend a lot of time talking about that and the implications of that approach being different from what others are taking.
turns.zh: 其中一部分是研究出能够实现你所说的个性化的技术。所以不是一个集中的神级 LLM，而是大量多样化的模型，针对特定需求、特定风格、特定业务、特定品牌等进行微调。同时让人们能够非常容易地为自己业务或作为创作者创建它们，以帮助你与粉丝互动。我认为这是一个清晰且有趣的产品方向，与你们、我或任何其他大公司所采取的方向相当不同。这也与这种开源方法非常契合，因为我们相信一种更社区导向、更民主的方式来构建产品和相关技术。我们不认为会有唯一正确的东西；我们认为应该有很多发展。所以这部分我认为会非常有趣，我们可以花很多时间讨论这一点，以及这种方法与其他公司不同的含义。

turns.en: But then there's a bunch of other simpler things that I think we're also going to do. Just going back to your question around how this finds its way into what we build: there are going to be a lot of simpler things around, okay, you post photos on Instagram and Facebook and in WhatsApp and Messenger, and you want the photos to look as good as possible. So having an AI that you can just take a photo and then just tell it, okay, I want to edit this thing or describe this, I think we're going to have tools that are just way better than what we've historically had on this. And that's more in the image and media generation side than the large language model side, but it all kind of plays off advances in the same space. So there are a lot of tools that I think are just going to get built into every one of our products. I think every single thing that we do is going to basically get evolved in this direction. Right, it's like in the future, if you're advertising on our services, do you need to make your own kind of ad creative? No, you'll just tell us, okay, I'm a dog walker and I'm willing to walk people's dogs, help me find the right people, and create the ad unit that will perform the best, and give an objective to the system, and it just kind of connects you with the right people.
turns.zh: 但还有很多其他更简单的事情我认为我们也会做。回到你关于这如何融入我们构建的产品的问题：会有很多更简单的事情，比如你在 Instagram、Facebook、WhatsApp 和 Messenger 上发布照片，你希望照片看起来尽可能好。所以有一个 AI，你可以拍张照片然后告诉它，好的，我想编辑这个或描述这个，我认为我们将拥有比以往好得多的工具。这更多属于图像和媒体生成方面，而不是大语言模型方面，但都得益于同一领域的进步。所以我认为很多工具将被内置到我们的每个产品中。我认为我们做的每一件事基本上都会朝这个方向进化。没错，就像未来，如果你在我们的服务上做广告，你需要自己制作广告创意吗？不，你只需告诉我们，好的，我是一个遛狗的人，我愿意遛别人的狗，帮我找到合适的人，并创建表现最好的广告单元，给系统一个目标，它就会把你和合适的人连接起来。

turns.en: Well, that's a super powerful idea of generating the language almost like rigorous A/B testing for you that works to find the best customer for your thing. I mean, to me, advertisement when done well just finds a good match between a human being and a thing that will make that human being happy. Yeah, totally, and do that as efficiently as possible. When it's done well, people actually like it. I think that there's a lot of examples where it's not done well, it's annoying, and I think that's what kind of gives it a bad rap. But yeah, a lot of the stuff is possible today. I mean, obviously A/B testing stuff is built into a lot of these frameworks. The thing that's new is having technology that can generate the ideas for you about what to A/B test. Something that's exciting.
turns.zh: 这是一个非常强大的想法，几乎像严格的 A/B 测试一样为你生成语言，从而找到最适合你产品的人。对我来说，广告做得好就是在人和能让他快乐的东西之间找到好的匹配。是的，完全正确，并且尽可能高效地做到这一点。当它做得好时，人们实际上会喜欢它。我认为有很多例子表明它做得不好，很烦人，我认为这就是它名声不好的原因。但是的，很多东西今天已经可以实现。我的意思是，显然 A/B 测试已经内置在很多框架中。新的是拥有能为你生成 A/B 测试想法的技术。这很令人兴奋。

turns.en: So this will just be across everything that we're doing, right? All the metaverse stuff that we're doing. It's like you want to create worlds in the future, you'll just describe them and then it'll create the code for you. So natural language becomes the interface we use for all the ways we interact with the computer, with the digital world.
turns.zh: 所以这将贯穿我们正在做的一切，对吧？我们正在做的所有元宇宙相关的事情。就像未来你想创建世界，你只需描述它们，它就会为你生成代码。所以自然语言成为我们与计算机、数字世界交互的界面。

turns.en: Yeah, yeah, totally. Yeah, which is what everyone can do using natural language, and with translation you can do it in any kind of language. I mean, for the personalization is really, really, really interesting. Yeah, it unlocks so many possible things. I mean, I for one look forward to creating a copy of myself. I know we talked about this last time, but since last time this becomes how we're closer, much closer. Like I could literally just having interact with some of these language models, I can see the absurd situation where I'll have a...
turns.zh: 是的，完全正确。是的，每个人都可以用自然语言做到这一点，而且通过翻译，你可以用任何语言做到。我的意思是，个性化真的非常非常有趣。是的，它解锁了太多可能的事情。我个人很期待创建自己的副本。我知道我们上次谈过这个，但自从上次以来，我们离这个目标更近了，非常近了。就像我实际上只是与一些语言模型互动，我就能看到荒谬的情况，我会有一个……

turns.en: Large language model, and I'll have to have a conversation with him about like, 'Hey listen, you're just getting out of line,' and having a conversation where you fine-tune that thing to be a little bit more respectful or something like this. I mean, that's going to be the — that seems like an amazing product for businesses, for humans. Not just the assistant that's facing the individual, but the assistant that represents the individual to the public, both directions. There's basically a layer that is the AI system through which you interact with the outside world that has humans in it. That's really interesting, and you have social networks that connect billions of people. It seems like a heck of a large-scale place to test some of this stuff out.
turns.zh: 大型语言模型，我得跟它谈一谈，比如“嘿，你有点出格了”，然后通过对话把它微调得更尊重人一些。我觉得这对企业和个人来说都是一个了不起的产品。不仅是面向个人的助手，还有代表个人面向公众的助手，双向的。基本上就是有一个 AI 系统层，你通过它和外部世界（包括人类）互动。这很有意思，而且你拥有连接数十亿人的社交网络，这似乎是一个大规模测试这些东西的好地方。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, I think part of the reason why creators will want to do this is because they already have the communities on our services. And a lot of the interface for this stuff today are chat-type interfaces, and between WhatsApp and Messenger, I think that those are great ways to interact with people.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为创作者愿意这么做的部分原因是他们已经在我们的服务上有了社区。如今这类东西的很多界面都是聊天式的，而 WhatsApp 和 Messenger 我认为是与他人互动的绝佳方式。

turns.en: Some of this is philosophy, but do you see a near-term future where you have some of the people you're friends with are AI systems on these social networks, on Facebook, on Instagram, even on WhatsApp, having conversations where some heterogeneous, some is human, some is AI?
turns.zh: 这有些哲学意味，但你认为在不久的将来，你在这些社交网络（Facebook、Instagram，甚至 WhatsApp）上的一些朋友会是 AI 系统，进行一些混合对话，部分是人类，部分是 AI 吗？

turns.en: I think we'll get to that. You know, if only just empirically, looking at Microsoft released this thing called Xiaoice several years ago in China. It was a pre-chatbot technology that was a lot simpler than what's possible today, and I think it was like tens of millions of people were using this and really became quite attached and built relationships with it. And there are services today like Replika where people are doing things like that. So I think there's certainly needs for companionship that people have, older people, and I think most people probably don't have as many friends as they would like to have. If you look at some interesting demographic studies around that, the average person has fewer close friends today than 15 years ago. And that gets to the core thing that I think about in terms of building services that help connect people. So I think you'll get tools that help people connect with each other — that's going to be the primary thing that we want to do. So you can imagine AI assistants that do a better job of reminding you when it's your friend's birthday and how you could celebrate them. Right now we have the little box in the corner of the website that tells you whose birthday it is, but at some level you don't want to just send everyone the same note saying 'Happy Birthday' with an emoji. So having something that's more of a social assistant in that sense, that can update you on what's going on in their life and how you can reach out to them effectively, help you be a better friend — I think that's super powerful too.
turns.zh: 我觉得我们会走到那一步。仅凭经验来看，微软几年前在中国发布了“小冰”，那是一种更简单的预聊天机器人技术，当时就有数千万人在使用，并且对它产生了依恋，建立了关系。现在有像 Replika 这样的服务，人们也在做类似的事情。所以我认为人们确实有陪伴的需求，比如老年人，而且大多数人可能没有他们想要的那么多朋友。一些有趣的人口统计研究显示，现在的人均亲密朋友数量比 15 年前更少。这触及了我思考的核心——构建帮助人们连接的服务。所以你会得到帮助人们相互连接的工具，这是我们要做的主要事情。你可以想象 AI 助手能更好地提醒你朋友的生日以及如何庆祝。现在我们在网站角落有个小框告诉你谁的生日到了，但在某种程度上，你不想给每个人都发一条同样的“生日快乐”加个表情符号。所以拥有一个更像社交助手的东西，能告诉你他们最近怎么样，如何有效地联系他们，帮助你成为更好的朋友——我认为这也非常强大。

turns.en: Beyond that, there are all these different flavors of kind of personal AIs that I think could exist. So I think an assistant is sort of the simplest one to wrap your head around, but a mentor or a life coach, someone who can give you advice, who's maybe a bit of a cheerleader who can help pick you up through all the challenges that we all go through on a daily basis. There's probably some role for something like that. And then you can probably go through a lot of the different types of functional relationships that people have in their life. I would bet that there will be companies out there that take a crack at a lot of these things. So I think it's part of the interesting innovation that's going to exist. Like education tutors — I just look at my kids learning to code, they love it, but they get stuck on a question and have to wait till I can help answer it or someone else who knows. In the future, they'll just have a coding assistant that is designed to be perfect for teaching a five and a seven-year-old how to code, and they'll be able to ask questions all the time, and it'll be extremely patient, never get annoyed at them. I think there are all these different kinds of relationships or functional relationships that we have in our lives that are really interesting. And I think one of the big questions is: is this all going to just get bucketed into one singular AI? I just don't think so.
turns.zh: 除此之外，还有各种不同种类的个人 AI 可能存在。我认为助手是最容易理解的一种，但还有导师或人生教练，可以给你建议，有点像拉拉队长，帮你振作起来应对日常挑战。这类东西可能有一些作用。然后你可以列举人们生活中各种功能性的关系。我敢打赌会有公司尝试做这些事情。所以我认为这是未来有趣创新的一部分。比如教育导师——我看我的孩子学编程，他们很喜欢，但遇到问题就得等我或别人来解答。未来他们会有个编程助手，专门设计来教五岁和七岁孩子编程，可以随时提问，而且非常有耐心，永远不会对他们不耐烦。我认为我们生活中所有这些不同类型的关系或功能性关系都非常有趣。其中一个重要问题是：所有这些是否会归结为一个单一的 AI？我不这么认为。

ts.sec: Long-term effects of AI on human communication

turns.en: Do you think about this? Actually, a question from Reddit: 'What are the long-term effects of human communication when people can talk with — in quotes — talk with others through a chatbot that augments their language automatically, rather than developing social skills by making mistakes and learning? Will people just communicate by grunts in a generation?' Do you think about long-term effects at scale, the integration of AI in our social interaction?
turns.zh: 你考虑过这个吗？实际上，Reddit 上有个问题：“当人们可以通过聊天机器人自动增强语言来与他人‘交谈’，而不是通过犯错和学习来发展社交技能时，人类沟通的长期影响是什么？一代人之后，人们会不会只用咕哝声交流？”你考虑过大规模长期影响吗，AI 融入我们的社交互动？

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, I think it's mostly good. That question was sort of framed in a negative way, but we were talking before about language models helping you communicate — like language translation helps you communicate with people who don't speak your language. At some level, what all this social technology is doing is helping people express themselves better to people in situations where they would otherwise have a hard time doing that. So part of it might be, okay, because you speak a language I don't know — that's a pretty basic one. I don't think people are going to look at that and say it's sad that we have the capacity to do that because I should have just learned your language. That's a pretty high bar. But overall, there are all these impediments, and language is an imperfect way for people to express thoughts and ideas. It's one of the best we have — we have art, we have code — but language is also a mapping of the way you think, the way you see the world, the way who you are. And one of the applications I've recently talked to a person who's actually in a Jitu...
turns.zh: 是的，我认为总体上是好的。那个问题有点负面，但我们之前谈到语言模型帮助沟通——比如语言翻译帮你和不说你语言的人交流。在某种程度上，所有这些社交技术都在帮助人们更好地表达自己，尤其是在他们本来很难做到的情况下。所以部分原因可能是，因为你说的语言我不懂——这是很基本的一点。我不认为人们会看着翻译说“真可悲，我们居然需要这个，我应该直接学你的语言”，那要求太高了。但总的来说，存在各种障碍，语言是人们表达思想和想法的不完美方式。它是最好的方式之一——我们有艺术、有代码——但语言也是你思维方式、世界观和自我的映射。最近我和一个来自 Jitu 的人聊过一个应用...

turns.en: Instructor, um, he said that when he emails parents about their son and daughter, um, that they can improve their discipline in class and so on, he often finds that he comes off a bit more of an asshole than he would like, so he uses GPT to translate his original email into a nicer email. We hear this all the time. A lot of creators on our services tell us that one of the most stressful things, um, is basically negotiating deals with brands and stuff, like the business side of it, because they're like, I mean, they do their thing, right? And, you know, the creators, they're excellent at what they do, and they just want to connect with their community, but then they get really stressed. You know, they go into their DMs and they see some brand wants to do something with them, and they don't quite know how to negotiate or how to push back respectfully. And, um, so I think building a tool that can actually allow them to do that well — the one simple thing that I think is just an interesting thing that we've heard from a bunch of people that they'd be interested in. But going back to the broader idea, um, I don't know. I mean, you, I just — Priscilla and I just had our third daughter, um, a couple — and, you know, it's like one of the saddest things in the world is like sing your baby cry, right? But like, it's like, what, why is that, right? It's like, well, because babies don't generally have much capacity to tell you what they care about otherwise, right? It's not actually just babies, right? It's, um, you know, my 5-year-old daughter cries too because she sometimes has a hard time expressing, you know, what, um, matters to her. And I was thinking about that and I was like, well, you know, actually a lot of adults get very frustrated too because they can't — they have a hard time expressing things in a way that, going back to some of the early themes, that maybe is something that, you know, was a mistake or maybe they have pride or something. Like all these things get in the way. So I don't know. I think that all these different technologies that can help us navigate the social complexity and actually be able to better express what we're feeling and thinking — I think that's generally all good. And, um, there are always these concerns like, okay, are people going to have worse memories because you have Google to look things up? And I think in general, a generation later, you don't look back and lament that. I think it's just like, wow, we have so much more capacity to do so much more now. And I think that that'll be the case here too. You can allocate those cognitive capabilities to like deeper, more nuanced thought.
turns.zh: 有位老师跟我说，他给家长发邮件，说他们的孩子在课堂上可以改进纪律之类的事情，他经常发现自己写出来的语气比预想中更冲，所以他就用 GPT 把原来的邮件翻译成更温和的版本。我们经常听到这类事。很多在我们平台上的创作者告诉我们，最让他们压力大的事情之一就是跟品牌谈合作这类商业事务。因为他们擅长的是创作本身，他们只想跟社区互动，但一看到私信里有品牌想合作，就不知道该怎么谈判或如何得体地拒绝。所以我觉得，如果能做一个工具帮他们做好这件事——一个简单但有趣的点子，我们听很多人说过他们感兴趣。但回到更宽泛的想法，嗯，我不知道。我和普莉希拉刚有了第三个女儿，你知道，世界上最让人难过的事情之一就是听到婴儿哭，对吧？但为什么呢？因为婴儿通常没有能力告诉你他们关心什么。其实不只是婴儿，我五岁的女儿也会哭，因为她有时很难表达出什么对她重要。我就在想，其实很多成年人也会很沮丧，因为他们也很难表达——回到之前的一些主题——也许是因为犯了错，或者因为自尊心之类的东西在作祟。所以我觉得，所有这些能帮助我们应对社交复杂性、更好地表达感受和想法的技术，总的来说都是好事。当然，总有人担心，比如有了谷歌搜索，人们的记忆力会不会变差？但我觉得，一代人之后，没有人会回头哀叹这件事。大家只会觉得，哇，我们现在有能力做更多事情了。我认为这里也是一样，你可以把那些认知能力分配到更深入、更细致的思考上。

turns.en: Yeah, but it's changed. So with, uh, just like with Google search, the additional language models, large language models, you basically don't have to remember nearly as much. Just like with Stack Overflow for programming, now that these language models can generate code, right? I mean, I find that I write maybe 80% to 90% of the code I write is, uh, now generated first and then edited. I mean, so you don't have to remember how to write specifics of different functions. Oh, but that's great. And it's also not just the specific coding. I mean, in the context of a large company like this, I think before an engineer can sit down to code, they first need to figure out all of the libraries and dependencies that, you know, tens of thousands of people have written before them. And, um, you know, one of the things that I'm excited about that we're working on is it's not just, um, you know, tools that help engineers code; it's tools that can help summarize the whole knowledge base and help people be able to navigate all the internal information. I think that that's, um, in the experiments that I've done with this stuff, I mean, that's on the public stuff. You just, you know, ask, ask, um, one of these models to build you a script that does anything, and it basically already understands what the best libraries are to do that thing and pulls them in automatically. It's, I think, super powerful. That was always the most annoying part of coding — that you had to spend all this time actually figuring out what the resources were that you were supposed to import before you could actually start building the thing.
turns.zh: 是的，但情况已经变了。就像谷歌搜索一样，有了额外的语言模型、大语言模型，你基本上不需要记住那么多东西了。就像编程中的 Stack Overflow，现在这些语言模型可以生成代码了。我发现我写的代码中，大概 80% 到 90% 都是先生成再编辑的。所以你不需要记住不同函数的具体写法。这很棒。而且不仅仅是具体的编码，在这样的大公司里，工程师在开始编码之前，首先需要搞清楚成千上万人之前写过的所有库和依赖关系。我们正在做的一件事让我很兴奋，那就是不仅仅是帮助工程师编码的工具，而是能够总结整个知识库、帮助人们浏览所有内部信息的工具。在我做的实验中，即使是公开的内容，你只要让这些模型给你写一个能做任何事情的脚本，它基本上已经知道做那件事最好的库是什么，并自动引入它们。我觉得这非常强大。编程中最烦人的部分一直就是，你得花大量时间搞清楚在真正开始构建之前应该导入哪些资源。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, there's of course the flip side of that. I think for the most part it's positive, but the flip side is, if you outsource that thinking to an AI model, you might miss nuanced mistakes and bugs. You lose the skill to find those bugs, and those bugs might be — the code looks very convincingly right, but it's actually wrong in a very subtle way. But that's the tradeoff that we face as human civilization when we build more and more powerful tools. When we stand on the shoulders of taller and taller giants, we can do more, but then we forget how to do all the stuff that they did. It's a weird tradeoff.
turns.zh: 是的，当然也有另一面。我认为大部分是积极的，但另一面是，如果你把思考外包给 AI 模型，你可能会错过细微的错误和 bug。你会失去发现这些 bug 的技能，而这些 bug 可能是——代码看起来非常正确，但实际上在很微妙的地方出错了。但这就是我们人类文明在构建越来越强大的工具时所面临的权衡。当我们站在越来越高的巨人肩膀上时，我们能做更多，但也会忘记他们所做的一切。这是一个奇怪的权衡。

turns.en: Yeah, I agree. I mean, I think it is very valuable in your life to be able to do basic things too.
turns.zh: 是的，我同意。我认为在生活中能够做基本的事情也是非常有价值的。

ts.sec: Concerns about bots on social networks

turns.en: Do you worry about some of the, um, concerns of bots being present on social networks more and more humanlike bots that are not necessarily trying to do a good thing, or they might be explicitly trying to do a bad thing like phishing scams? Yeah, like social engineering, all that kind of stuff, which has always been a very difficult problem for social networks, but now it's becoming almost a more and more difficult problem.
turns.zh: 你是否担心社交网络上出现越来越多像真人一样的机器人，它们不一定在做好事，或者可能明确在做坏事，比如钓鱼诈骗？是的，社交工程之类的东西，这对社交网络来说一直是个非常棘手的问题，但现在它变得越来越难了。

turns.en: Well, I think there are a few different parts of this. So one is, there are all these harms that we need to basically fight against and prevent. And that's been, you know, a lot of our focus over the last, you know, five or seven years, is basically ramping up very sophisticated AI systems — not generative AI systems, more kind of classical AI systems — to be able to, um, you know, categorize and classify and identify: okay, this post looks like it's promoting terrorism, this one is, you know, like exploiting children, this one is, um, looks like it might be trying to incite violence, this one's intellectual property violation. So there's like 18 different categories of violating, kind of, harmful content that we've had to build specific systems to be able to track. And, um, I think it's certainly the case that advances in generative AI will test those. But at least so far, it's been the case, and I'm optimistic that it will continue to be the case, that we will be able to bring more computing power to bear to have even stronger AIs that can help defend against those things. So, um, we've had to deal with some adversarial issues before, right? I mean, for some things like hate speech, it's like people aren't generally getting a lot more sophisticated. Like the average person who, let's say, you know, someone's saying some kind of racist thing, right? It's like they're not necessarily getting more sophisticated at being racist, right? It just — okay, so that the system —
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为这有几个不同的方面。首先，我们需要对抗和预防所有这些危害。过去五到七年，我们的重点之一就是大力建设非常复杂的 AI 系统——不是生成式 AI，而是更传统的 AI 系统——来分类和识别：这篇帖子看起来在宣扬恐怖主义，这篇在剥削儿童，这篇可能试图煽动暴力，这篇侵犯知识产权。总共有 18 种不同的违规有害内容类别，我们不得不建立专门的系统来追踪。我认为生成式 AI 的进步肯定会考验这些系统。但至少到目前为止，情况是这样，而且我乐观地认为未来也会如此：我们将能够投入更多的算力，拥有更强大的 AI 来帮助防御这些威胁。我们以前也处理过一些对抗性问题。比如仇恨言论，人们通常不会变得更狡猾。比如一个说种族主义言论的人，他们不一定在种族主义方面变得更狡猾。所以系统——

ts.sec: Coordinated Inauthentic Behavior and Adversarial Attacks

turns.en: You can just find, but then there are other adversaries who actually are very sophisticated, like nation states doing things. And we find you, whether it's Russia or just different countries that are basically standing up these networks of bots or inauthentic accounts—that's what we call them because they're not necessarily bots; some of them could actually be real people who are masquerading as other people, but they're acting in a coordinated way. And some of that behavior has gotten very sophisticated and very adversarial. So each iteration, every time we find something and stop them, they kind of evolve their behavior. They don't just pack up their bags and go home. At some point they might decide doing it on Meta services is not worth it; they'll go do it on someone else if it's easier to do it in another place. But we have a fair amount of experience dealing with even those kind of adversarial attacks where they just keep on getting better and better. And I do think that as long as we can keep on putting more compute power against it, and if we're kind of one of the leaders in developing some of these AI models, I'm quite optimistic that we're going to be able to keep on pushing against the normal categories of harm that you talk about: fraud, scams, spam, IP violations, things like that.
turns.zh: 你能找到，但还有一些对手非常老练，比如国家行为体。我们发现了，无论是俄罗斯还是其他一些国家，它们基本上在搭建这些机器人或虚假账号网络——我们称之为“虚假账号”，因为它们不一定是机器人；有些可能是真人冒充他人，但以协调方式行动。其中一些行为已经变得非常复杂且极具对抗性。每次我们找到并阻止它们，它们就会演化行为。它们不会就此收手。有时它们可能觉得在 Meta 服务上做不值得，就会去别处，如果其他地方更容易的话。但我们有相当多的经验来应对这类不断升级的对抗性攻击。而且我确实认为，只要我们能够持续投入更多算力，并且我们在开发一些 AI 模型方面处于领先地位，我相当乐观地认为我们能够持续打击你提到的那些常见危害类别：欺诈、诈骗、垃圾信息、知识产权侵权等等。

turns.en: What about creating narratives and controversy? To me, it's kind of amazing how a small collection of—yeah, what did you say—inauthentic accounts, so it could be bots, but yeah, I mean we have this funny name for it, but we call it coordinated inauthentic behavior. Yeah, it's kind of incredible how a small collection of folks can create narratives, create stories, especially if they're viral, especially if they have an element that can catalyze the virality of the narrative.
turns.zh: 那制造叙事和争议呢？对我来说，一小撮——嗯，你刚才说的——虚假账号，可能是机器人，但我们有个有趣的名字，叫“协调性虚假行为”。确实，一小群人就能制造叙事、编造故事，尤其是如果它们能病毒式传播，特别是如果它们有能催化叙事病毒性的元素，这挺不可思议的。

turns.en: Yeah, I think the question is you have to be very specific about what is bad about it, right? Because I think a set of people coming together or organically bouncing ideas off each other and a narrative comes out of that is not necessarily a bad thing by itself, if it's kind of authentic and organic. That's a lot of what happens and how culture gets created and how art gets created and a lot of good stuff. So that's why we've kind of focused on this sense of coordinated inauthentic behavior. So it's like if you have a network of—whether it's bots, some people masquerading as different accounts—but you have someone pulling the strings behind it and trying to act as if this is a more organic set of behavior but really it's not, it's just one coordinated thing that seems problematic to me. I mean, I don't think people should be able to have coordinated networks and not disclose it as such. But that again, we've been able to deploy pretty sophisticated AI and counterterrorism groups and things like that to identify a fair number of these coordinated inauthentic networks of accounts and take them down. We continue to do that. And I think it's one thing that if you told me 20 years ago, 'All right, you're starting this website to help people connect at a college, and in the future you're going to be part of your organization—it's going to be a counterterrorism organization with AI to find coordinated inauthentic behavior'—I would have thought that was pretty wild. But that's part of where we are. But look, I think that these questions that you're pushing on now—this is actually where I'd guess most of the challenge around AI will be for the foreseeable future. I think there's a lot of debate around things like 'Is this going to create existential risk to humanity?' And those are very hard things to disprove one way or another. My own intuition is that the point where we become close to superintelligence is—it's just really unclear to me that the current technology is going to get there without another set of significant advances. But that doesn't mean there's no danger. I think the danger is basically amplifying the kind of known set of harms that people or sets of accounts can do, and we just need to make sure that we really focus on doing that as well as possible. So that's definitely a big focus for me.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为问题在于你必须非常具体地指出哪里不好，对吧？因为我觉得一群人聚在一起，或者自然地碰撞想法，然后产生一个叙事，这本身不一定是坏事，如果它是真实和自然的。很多文化创造和艺术创作就是这样发生的，还有很多好东西。所以我们才专注于“协调性虚假行为”这个概念。也就是说，如果你有一个网络——无论是机器人，还是有些人冒充不同账号——但有人在背后操纵，试图让它看起来更自然，但实际上并非如此，只是一个协调性的东西，那在我看来就有问题。我的意思是，我认为人们不应该能够拥有协调性网络而不披露这一点。但同样，我们已经能够部署相当先进的 AI 和反恐团队等，来识别相当多的这类协调性虚假账号网络并予以清除。我们一直在这样做。而且我觉得，如果你 20 年前告诉我，‘好吧，你创办这个网站是为了帮助大学里的人们联系，未来你的组织的一部分将是一个用 AI 寻找协调性虚假行为的反恐组织’——我会觉得那太疯狂了。但这就是我们现在的处境。不过，我认为你现在追问的这些问题——实际上，我猜测在可预见的未来，AI 的大部分挑战都将集中于此。关于‘这会不会给人类带来生存风险’之类的问题有很多争论。这些很难从任何一方证伪。我个人的直觉是，我们接近超级智能的那个点——我不太清楚当前的技术是否能在没有另一系列重大突破的情况下达到那里。但这并不意味着没有危险。我认为危险基本上在于放大了人们或账号群体所能造成的已知危害类型，我们只需要确保我们真正专注于尽可能好地应对这一点。所以这绝对是我关注的重点。

turns.en: Well, you can basically use large language models as an assistant of how to cause harm on a social network. So you can ask it a question—you know, Meta has very impressive coordinated inauthentic account fighting capabilities—'How do I do coordinated inauthentic account creation where Meta doesn't detect it?' Like literally ask that question. And basically there's this kind of—I mean, that's what OpenAI showed that they're concerned with those questions. Perhaps you can comment on your approach to it: how to do a kind of moderation on the output of those models so that it can't be used to help you coordinate harm in the full definition of what harm means.
turns.zh: 嗯，你基本上可以把大语言模型当作如何在社交网络上造成危害的助手。所以你可以问它一个问题——你知道，Meta 有非常强大的协调性虚假账号打击能力——‘我如何创建 Meta 检测不到的协调性虚假账号？’就像直接问那个问题。基本上，这就是 OpenAI 所展示的他们担心这类问题。也许你可以评论一下你的方法：如何对这些模型的输出进行某种审核，使其不能被用来帮助你协调危害——在危害的完整定义下。

turns.en: Yeah, and that's a lot of the fine-tuning and the alignment training that we do. Basically, when we ship AI across our products, a lot of what we're trying to make sure is that you can't ask it to help you commit a crime, right? So I think training it to understand that—and it's not like any of these systems are ever going to be 100% perfect—but just making it so that this isn't an easier way to go about doing something bad than the next best alternative. I mean, people still have Google, right? You still have search engines. So the information is out there. And for what we see, like for nation states or these actors that are trying to pull off these large coordinated inauthentic networks to influence different things, at some point when we just make it very difficult, they do just try to use other services instead. It's just like if you can make it more expensive for them to do it on your service, then people go elsewhere. And I think that's the bar. It's not like 'Are you ever going to be perfect at finding every adversary who tries to attack you?' You try to get as close to that as possible, but I think really, economically, what you're just trying to do is make it inefficient for them to go after that. But there are also complicated questions of what is and isn't harm, what is and isn't misinformation. So this is one of the things that Wikipedia has also tried to face. I remember asking GPT about whether the virus leaked from a...
turns.zh: 是的，这很大程度上就是我们做的微调和对齐训练。基本上，当我们在产品中部署 AI 时，我们主要确保的是你不能让它帮你犯罪，对吧？所以我认为训练它理解这一点——而且这些系统永远不会 100% 完美——但只是让它不成为做坏事的更容易的途径，相比次优选择。我的意思是，人们还有谷歌，对吧？你还有搜索引擎。所以信息就在那里。而且根据我们的观察，对于国家行为体或那些试图搭建大型协调性虚假网络以影响各种事务的参与者，一旦我们让事情变得非常困难，他们确实会尝试使用其他服务。就像如果你能让他们在你的服务上做这件事的成本更高，他们就会去别处。我认为这就是标准。不是说‘你能完美地找到每一个试图攻击你的对手吗？’你尽可能接近完美，但我认为实际上，从经济角度来说，你只是试图让他们攻击你变得低效。但还有一些复杂的问题：什么是危害，什么不是；什么是虚假信息，什么不是。这也是 Wikipedia 试图面对的问题之一。我记得问过 GPT 病毒是否从……泄露。

ts.sec: Nuance in content moderation and AI systems

turns.en: Lab or not and the answer provided was a very nuanced one and a well-sited one, almost dare I say well thought out one, balanced. I would hate for that nuance to be lost through the process of moderation. Wikipedia does a good job on that particular thing too, but from pressures from governments and institutions, you could see some of that nuance and depth of information, facts, and wisdom be lost. Absolutely, and that's a scary thing. Some of the magic, some of the edges, the rough edges might be lost to the process of moderation of AI systems. So how do you get that right?
turns.zh: 实验室与否，给出的答案非常微妙，而且引证得当，我甚至敢说深思熟虑、平衡周全。我担心这种微妙之处会在审核过程中丢失。维基百科在那方面做得不错，但在政府和机构的压力下，你会看到一些信息、事实和智慧的微妙与深度丢失了。确实，这很可怕。一些魔力、一些棱角、粗糙的边缘可能会在 AI 系统的审核过程中丢失。那么，如何把这件事做对呢？

turns.en: I really agree with what you're pushing on. I mean, the core shape of the problem is that there are some harms that I think everyone agrees are bad, right? So sexual exploitation of children, like you're not going to get many people who think that type of thing should be allowed on any service, right? And that's something that we face and try to push off as much as possible. You know, terrorism, inciting violence, right? It's like we went through a bunch of these types of harms before. But then I do think that you get to a set of harms where there is more social debate around it. So misinformation, I think, has been a really tricky one because there are things that are kind of obviously false, right, that are maybe factual but may not be harmful. Since like, all right, are you going to censor someone for just being wrong? If there's no kind of harm implication of what they're doing, I think that there's a bunch of real issues and challenges there.
