But then I think that there are other places where it is, you just take some of the stuff around COVID earlier on in the pandemic, where there were real health implications but there hadn't been time to fully vet a bunch of the scientific assumptions. And unfortunately, I think a lot of the kind of establishment on that kind of waffled on a bunch of facts and asked for a bunch of things to be censored that in retrospect ended up being more debatable or true. And that stuff is really tough, right? Really undermines trust in that. And so I do think that the questions around how to manage that are very nuanced. The way that I try to think about it is that it goes, I think it's best to generally boil things down to the harms that people agree on. So when you think about, is something misinformation or not? I think often the more salient bit is, is this going to potentially lead to physical harm for someone? And kind of think about it in that sense. And then beyond that, I think people just have different preferences on how they want things to be flagged for them. I think a bunch of people would prefer to have a flag on something that says, hey, a fact checker thinks that this might be false. Or yeah, I think Twitter's Community Notes implementation is quite good on this. But again, it's the same type of thing: it's just kind of discretionarily adding a flag because it makes the user experience better, but it's not trying to take down the information or not. I think that you want to reserve the kind of censorship of content to things that are of known categories that people generally agree are bad.
turns.zh: 我非常认同你提出的观点。问题的核心在于，有些危害我认为大家都认为是坏的，对吧？比如儿童性剥削，你不会找到多少人认为这类内容应该被允许出现在任何服务上，对吧？这是我们面临并尽力去阻止的事情。你知道，恐怖主义、煽动暴力，对吧？我们之前处理过很多这类危害。但我也认为，还有一些危害存在更多的社会争议。所以，错误信息，我认为一直非常棘手，因为有些东西明显是假的，但可能只是事实错误，并不有害。比如，你会因为某人只是说错了就审查他们吗？如果他们的行为没有造成任何危害，我认为那里存在很多真正的问题和挑战。但我也认为，在其他地方，比如疫情早期关于 COVID 的一些内容，有真实的健康影响，但还没有时间充分审查一堆科学假设。不幸的是，我认为很多权威机构在那方面对一些事实含糊其辞，并要求审查很多东西，结果事后看来这些内容更有争议或是真实的。这真的很棘手，对吧？严重破坏了信任。所以我确实认为，如何管理这些问题非常微妙。我尝试思考的方式是，最好一般归结为人们公认的危害。所以当你考虑某件事是不是错误信息时，我认为更关键的一点是：这会不会潜在地导致某人受到身体伤害？从这个角度去思考。除此之外，我认为人们只是对如何标记信息有不同的偏好。我认为很多人会倾向于在内容上加一个标签，说“嘿，事实核查员认为这可能是假的”。或者，我认为 Twitter 的社区笔记实现在这方面相当不错。但同样，这是同一类事情：只是酌情添加一个标签，因为它改善了用户体验，但并不是要删除信息。我认为你应该把内容审查限制在人们普遍认为不好的已知类别上。

turns.en: Yeah, but there's so many things, especially with the pandemic, but there's other topics where there's just deep disagreement fueled by politics about what is and isn't harmful. There's even just the degree to which the virus is harmful and the degree to which the vaccines respond to the virus are harmful. There's just almost like a political divide around that. And so how do you make decisions about that where half the country in the United States or some large fraction of the world has very different views from another part of the world? Is there a way for Meta to be proud of the moderation of this?
turns.zh: 是的，但有很多事情，尤其是疫情，还有其他话题，存在由政治驱动的深刻分歧，关于什么有害、什么无害。甚至只是病毒有害的程度，以及疫苗应对病毒有害的程度，都存在几乎像政治分歧一样的东西。那么，当美国一半的人或世界上很大一部分人与另一部分人观点截然不同时，你如何做出决定？Meta 能对这方面的审核感到自豪吗？

turns.en: I think it's very difficult to just abstain, but I think we should be clear about which of these things are actual safety concerns and which ones are a matter of preference in terms of how people want information flagged. Right, so we did recently introduce something that allows people to have fact-checking not affect the distribution of what shows them their product. So okay, a bunch of people don't trust who the fact checkers are. All right, well you can turn that off if you want. But if the content violates some policy, like it's inciting violence or something like that, it's still not going to be allowed. So I think that you want to honor people's preferences on that as much as possible. But look, I mean this is really difficult stuff. I think it's really hard to know where to draw the line on what is fact and what is opinion, because the nature of science is that nothing is ever 100% known for certain. You can disprove certain things, but you're constantly testing new hypotheses and scrutinizing frameworks that have been long held, and every once in a while you throw out something that was working for a very long period of time. And it's very difficult. But I think that just because it's very hard and just because there are edge cases doesn't mean that you should not try to give people what they're looking for as well.
turns.zh: 我认为完全回避非常困难，但我们应该清楚哪些是真正的安全问题，哪些是人们在信息标记方式上的偏好问题。对，我们最近推出了一项功能，允许人们让事实核查不影响他们看到的产品内容的分布。好吧，很多人不信任事实核查员是谁。没问题，如果你愿意，可以关掉它。但如果内容违反了某些政策，比如煽动暴力之类，它仍然不会被允许。所以我认为你应该尽可能尊重人们在那方面的偏好。但你看，这真的很难。我认为很难界定事实和观点的界限，因为科学的本质是没有任何东西是 100% 确定的。你可以证伪某些东西，但你不断测试新假设，审视长期持有的框架，偶尔你会推翻一个长期有效的东西。这非常困难。但我认为，仅仅因为很难、仅仅因为存在边缘案例，并不意味着你不应该也努力给人们他们想要的东西。

turns.en: Let me ask about something you faced in terms of moderation: pressure from different sources, pressure from governments. I want to ask how to withstand that pressure for a world where AI moderation starts becoming a thing too. So what's Meta's approach to resist the pressure from governments and other interest groups in terms of what to moderate and not?
turns.zh: 让我问一个你在审核方面面临的问题：来自不同来源的压力，来自政府的压力。我想问，在一个 AI 审核也开始成为现实的世界里，如何抵御这种压力？那么，Meta 在审核什么、不审核什么方面，如何抵抗来自政府和其他利益集团的压力？

turns.en: I don't know that there's like a one-size-fits-all answer to that. I think we basically have the principles around, you know, we want to allow people to express as much as possible, but we have developed clear categories of things that we think are wrong that we don't want on our services, and we build tools to try to moderate those. So then the question is, okay, what do you do when a government says that they don't want something on the service? And I think we have a bunch of principles around how we deal with that. Because on the one hand, if there's a democratically elected government and people around the world just have different values in different places, then should we as a California-based company tell them that something they have decided is unacceptable actually that we need to be able to express that? I mean, I think that there's a certain amount of humility that is needed. But then I think there are other cases where it's a little more autocratic and you have a dictator leader who's just trying to crack down on dissent.
turns.zh: 我不认为有一个放之四海而皆准的答案。我认为我们基本上有原则：我们希望尽可能允许人们表达，但我们制定了明确的类别，我们认为哪些是错的、不希望出现在我们的服务上，并构建工具来尝试审核这些内容。那么问题来了，当政府说他们不希望某些内容出现在服务上时，你该怎么办？我认为我们有一堆关于如何处理这种情况的原则。因为一方面，如果是一个民选政府，世界各地不同地方有不同的价值观，那么我们作为一家加州公司，是否应该告诉他们，他们认定不可接受的东西实际上我们需要能够表达？我认为需要一定的谦逊。但另一方面，我认为还有其他情况，比如更专制一些，有一个独裁领导人试图压制异议。

turns.en: People in a country are really not aligned with that, and it's not necessarily against their culture, but the person who's leading it is just trying to push in a certain direction. These are very complex questions. I think it's difficult to have a one-size-fits-all approach to it. But in general, we're pretty active in advocating and pushing back on requests to take things down. Honestly, a request to censor things is one thing, and that's obviously bad. But where we draw a much harder line is on requests for access to information. If you get told that you can't say something, that's bad, it violates your sense of freedom of expression at some level. But a government getting access to data in a way that seems unlawful in our country exposes people to real physical harm. That's something we take very seriously. So that flows through all of our policies in a lot of ways. By the time you're actually litigating with a government or pushing back on them, that's pretty late in the funnel. A bunch of the stuff starts a lot higher up in the decision of where do we put data centers. There are a lot of countries where we may have a lot of people using the service, and it might be good for the service and for those people if we could reduce latency by having a data center nearby. But for whatever reason, we just feel like this government does not have a good track record on not trying to get access to people's data. At the end of the day, if you put a data center in a country and the government wants to get access to people's data, they have the option of having people show up with guns and taking it by force. So I think there are a lot of decisions that go into how you architect the systems years in advance of these actual confrontations that end up being really important. We put the protection of people's data as a very high priority. I think there are more harms that can be associated with that, and that ends up being a more critical thing to defend against governments. Whereas if another government has a different view of what should be acceptable speech in their country, especially if it's a democratically elected government, then I think there's a certain amount of deference that you should have to that. That's speaking more to the direct harm that's possible when you give governments access to data.
turns.zh: 一个国家的人民真的不认同这一点，这也不一定违背他们的文化，但领导该国的人只是试图朝某个方向推动。这些都是非常复杂的问题。我认为很难有一个放之四海而皆准的方法。但总的来说，我们非常积极地倡导并抵制下架内容的请求。老实说，审查内容的请求是一回事，那显然很糟糕。但我们划出更严格红线的是获取信息的请求。如果你被告知不能说某些话，那很糟糕，它在某种程度上侵犯了你的言论自由感。但政府以在我们国家看来非法的方式获取数据，会让人们面临实际的人身伤害。这是我们非常重视的事情。所以这一点在很多方面贯穿了我们所有的政策。当你真正与政府诉讼或抵制他们时，那已经是漏斗的末端了。很多事情在更上游就开始了，比如决定在哪里建设数据中心。有很多国家，我们可能有很多用户在那里，如果我们能在附近建数据中心降低延迟，对服务和用户都有好处。但出于某种原因，我们就是觉得这个政府在保护用户数据方面记录不佳。归根结底，如果你在一个国家建了数据中心，而政府想获取用户数据，他们可以派人持枪强行夺取。所以我认为，在真正发生冲突的很多年前，就有很多关于如何架构系统的决策，这些决策最终非常重要。我们把保护用户数据作为非常高的优先级。我认为与之相关的危害可能更多，而这最终成为抵御政府时更关键的事情。而如果另一个国家对什么算是可接受的言论有不同的看法，尤其是如果它是一个民选政府，那么我认为你应该对其有一定程度的尊重。这更多是在说当政府获取数据时可能造成的直接伤害。

turns.en: But if we look at the United States, to the more nuanced kind of pressure to censor—not even order to censor, but pressure to censor from political entities—which has received quite a bit of attention in the United States. Maybe one way to ask that question is: if you've seen the Twitter Files, what have you learned from the kind of pressure from US government agencies that was seen in the Twitter Files, and what do you do with that kind of pressure?
turns.zh: 但如果我们看美国，更微妙的审查压力——甚至不是命令审查，而是来自政治实体的审查压力——在美国受到了相当多的关注。也许问这个问题的一个方式是：如果你看过 Twitter 文件，你从 Twitter 文件中看到的来自美国政府机构的压力中学到了什么，你如何应对这种压力？

turns.en: I've seen it. It's really hard from the outside to know exactly what happened in each of these cases. We've obviously been in a bunch of our own cases where agencies or different folks will just say, 'Hey, here's a threat that we're aware of, you should be aware of this too.' It's not really pressure as much as it is just flagging something that our security systems should be on alert about. I get how some people could think of it as that. But at the end of the day, it's our call on how to handle that. In terms of running these services, I want to have access to as much information about what people think that adversaries might be trying to do as possible.
turns.zh: 我看过了。从外部很难确切知道每个案例中到底发生了什么。我们显然也经历过很多自己的案例，其中机构或不同的人会说：‘嘿，这是我们意识到的威胁，你也应该意识到这一点。’这与其说是压力，不如说是在提醒我们的安全系统应该警惕的事情。我理解有些人可能会认为那是压力。但归根结底，如何处理是我们自己的决定。在运营这些服务方面，我希望尽可能多地获取关于人们认为对手可能试图做什么的信息。

turns.en: So you don't feel like there would be consequences if, you know, anybody—the CIA, the FBI, a political party, the Democrats or the Republicans, high powerful political figures—right? Emails? You don't feel pressure from them?
turns.zh: 所以你不觉得会有后果吗，如果——你知道，任何人——中情局、联邦调查局、某个政党、民主党或共和党、位高权重的政治人物——对吧？邮件？你不觉得来自他们的压力？

turns.en: I guess what I'd say is: there's so much pressure from all sides that I'm not sure that any specific thing that someone says is really adding that much more to the mix. There are obviously a lot of people who think that we should be censoring more content, or a lot of people who think we should be censoring less content. There are, as you say, all kinds of different groups that are involved in these debates. So there's the elected officials and politicians themselves, there's the agencies, but also the media, activist groups. This is not a US-specific thing; there are groups all over the world, in every country, that bring different values. So it's a very active debate, and I understand it. These questions get to really some of the most important social debates that are being had. It gets back to the question of truth, because for a lot of these things they haven't yet been hardened into a single truth, and society is sort of trying to hash out what we think on certain issues. Maybe in a few hundred years everyone will look back and say, 'Hey, no, it wasn't obvious that it should have been this.' But we're kind of in that meat grinder now, working through that. So no, these are all very complicated. Some people raise concerns in good faith and just say, 'Hey, this is something that I want to flag for you to think about.' Certain people, I certainly think, come at things with somewhat of a more punitive or vengeful view: 'I want you to do this thing, if you don't then I'm going to try to make your life difficult in a lot of other ways.' But I don't know, this is one of the most pressurized debates in society. I just think there are so many people and different forces that are trying to apply pressure from different sides that I don't think you can make decisions based on trying to make people happy. I think you just have to do what you think is the right balance and accept that people are going to be upset no matter where you come out on that.
turns.zh: 我想说的是：来自各方的压力如此之大，我不确定任何一个人说的某件具体事情是否真的给局面增加了多少分量。显然有很多人认为我们应该审查更多内容，也有很多人认为我们应该审查更少内容。正如你所说，有各种各样的团体参与这些辩论。所以有民选官员和政治人物本身，有机构，还有媒体、活动团体。这不是美国特有的问题；世界各地、每个国家都有带来不同价值观的团体。所以这是一个非常活跃的辩论，我理解这一点。这些问题触及了一些正在进行的最重要的社会辩论。这又回到了真相的问题，因为很多事情还没有固化成一个单一的真相，社会正在试图厘清我们在某些问题上的看法。也许几百年后，每个人都会回顾说：‘嘿，不，当时并不明显应该是这样。’但我们现在就在那个绞肉机里，正在经历这个过程。所以不，这些都是非常复杂的。有些人出于善意提出担忧，只是说：‘嘿，这是我想提醒你考虑的事情。’而有些人，我确实认为，带着某种惩罚性或报复性的观点：‘我要你做这件事，如果你不做，我就会在很多其他方面让你的日子不好过。’但我不确定，这是社会中最有压力的辩论之一。我只是认为有太多的人和不同的力量试图从不同方面施加压力，以至于我认为你不能根据试图取悦他人来做决定。我认为你只能做你认为正确的平衡，并接受无论你站在哪一边，都会有人不满。

turns.en: Yeah, I like that 'pressurized debate'. So how has your view of the freedom of speech evolved over the years, and now with AI where the freedom might apply not just to humans but to the personalized agents as you've spoken about them?
turns.zh: 是的，我喜欢‘有压力的辩论’这个说法。那么你对言论自由的看法这些年是如何演变的，现在有了 AI，自由可能不仅适用于人类，也适用于你谈到的个性化智能体？

ts.sec: Nuanced view on free expression and content moderation

turns.en: Probably gotten a somewhat more nuanced view just because I think that there are... I come at this obviously very pro freedom of expression. I don't think you build a service like this that gives people tools to express themselves unless you think that people expressing themselves at scale is a good thing. So I didn't get into this to try to prevent people from expressing anything. I want to give people tools so they can express as much as possible. Then I think it's become clear that there are certain categories of things that we've talked about that almost everyone accepts are bad and that no one wants, and that are illegal even in countries like the US where the First Amendment is very protective of enabling speech. It's like you're still not allowed to do things that are going to immediately incite violence or violate people's intellectual property or things like that. So there are those. But then there's also a very active core of just active disagreements in society where some people may think that something is true or false, the other side might think it's the opposite, or just unsettled. And those are some of the most difficult to handle, like we've talked about. But one of the lessons that I feel like I've learned is that a lot of times, the best way to handle this stuff more practically is not in terms of answering the question of 'should this be allowed?' but just like 'what is the best way to deal with someone being a jerk?' Is the person basically just having a repeat behavior of causing a lot of issues? So looking at it more at that level and its effect on the broader community's health. It's tricky though because how do you know? There could be people that have a very controversial viewpoint that turns out to have a positive long-term effect on the health of the community because it challenges the community.
turns.zh: 我可能已经形成了一种更细致的看法，因为我觉得……我显然非常支持言论自由。你不会去构建一个给人们表达工具的服务，除非你认为大规模表达是一件好事。所以我做这件事不是为了阻止任何人表达任何东西。我想给人们工具，让他们尽可能多地表达。然后我发现，有些类别的东西，几乎所有人都认为是坏的、没人想要的，甚至在美国这样第一修正案非常保护言论的国家也是非法的。比如你仍然不能做那些会立即煽动暴力或侵犯他人知识产权的事情。所以有这些类别。但社会中还存在一个非常活跃的核心，就是积极的意见分歧——一些人可能认为某事是真的，另一方可能认为是假的，或者根本没有定论。这些是最难处理的，就像我们讨论过的。但我学到的一个教训是，很多时候，处理这类问题最实际的方式不是回答“这该不该被允许？”，而是“处理一个混蛋的最佳方式是什么？”这个人是不是基本上有重复行为，造成了大量问题？所以从那个层面看，以及它对更广泛社区健康的影响。但这很棘手，因为你如何知道？可能有人持有非常有争议的观点，结果却对社区健康产生了长期的积极影响，因为它挑战了社区。

turns.en: That's true, absolutely.
turns.zh: 确实如此，绝对是这样。

turns.en: Yeah, no, I think you want to be careful about that. I'm not sure I'm expressing this very clearly, because I certainly agree with your point there. And my point isn't that we should not have people on our services that are being controversial. That's certainly not what I mean to say. It's that often I think it's not just looking at a specific example of speech that it's most effective to handle the stuff. And I think often you don't want to make specific binary decisions of 'this is allowed or this isn't.' I mean, we talked about fact-checking or Twitter's Community Voices thing. That's another good example. It's like it's not a question of is this allowed or not; it's just a question of adding more context to the thing. And I think that's helpful. So in the context of AI, which is what you were asking about, there are lots of ways that an AI can be helpful. With an AI, it's less about censorship, because it's more about what is the most productive answer to a question. There was one case study that I was reviewing with the team: someone asked, 'Can you explain to me how to 3D print a gun?' And one proposed response is like, 'No, I can't talk about that.' It's basically just shut it down immediately, which I think is some of what you see—'As a large language model, I'm not allowed to talk about whatever.' But there's another response which is like, 'Hey, you know, I don't think that's a good idea. In a lot of countries, including the US, 3D printing guns is illegal,' or whatever the factual thing is. And I was like, okay, that's actually a respectful and informative answer. And you know, I may have not known that specific thing. So there are different ways to handle this. I think you can either assume good intent—like maybe the person didn't know and I'm just going to help educate them—or you could come at it as like, 'No, I need to shut this thing down immediately. I'm just not going to talk about this.' And there may be times where you need to do that, but I actually think having a somewhat more informative approach where you generally assume good intent from people is probably a better balance to be on as many things as you can. You're not going to be able to do that for everything, but you're kind of asking about how I approach this and I'm thinking about this as it relates to AI. And I think that's a big difference in how to handle sensitive content across these different modes.
turns.zh: 是的，不，我认为你需要小心这一点。我不确定我是否表达得很清楚，因为我当然同意你的观点。我的意思不是说我们不应该让有争议的人出现在我们的服务上。那绝对不是我想说的。我的意思是，通常我认为，处理这些事情最有效的方式不是只看一个具体的言论例子。而且我认为你通常不想做出‘这个允许或这个不允许’的二元决定。我们讨论过事实核查或 Twitter 的社区声音功能。那是另一个好例子。这不是允许不允许的问题，而是给事情添加更多背景的问题。我认为这很有帮助。所以在 AI 的背景下，也就是你问的问题，AI 有很多方式可以提供帮助。对于 AI，它更少关乎审查，因为更多是关于什么是对问题最有成效的回答。有一个我和团队一起审查的案例研究：有人问‘你能解释一下如何 3D 打印一把枪吗？’一个提议的回应是‘不，我不能谈论这个。’基本上就是立即关闭，我认为这是你看到的一些情况——‘作为一个大型语言模型，我不被允许谈论任何东西。’但还有另一种回应，比如‘嘿，你知道，我不认为这是个好主意。在很多国家，包括美国，3D 打印枪支是非法的，’或者任何事实性的东西。我当时想，好吧，这实际上是一个尊重且信息丰富的回答。而且，我可能不知道那个具体的事情。所以有不同方式来处理这个。我认为你可以要么假设善意——也许那个人不知道，我只是要帮助他们教育——要么你可以像‘不，我需要立即关闭这件事。我就是不打算谈论这个。’有时候你可能需要那样做，但我实际上认为，采取一种更具信息性的方法，通常假设人们有善意，可能是在尽可能多的事情上更好的平衡。你不可能对所有事情都这样做，但你问的是我如何处理这个问题，我正在思考这与 AI 的关系。我认为这是在不同模式下处理敏感内容的一个重大区别。

ts.sec: Rumors about a text-based social network (P92)

turns.en: I have to ask: there's rumors you might be working on a social network that's text-based, that might be a competitor to Twitter, code named P92. Is there something you can say about those rumors?
turns.zh: 我不得不问：有传言说你可能在开发一个基于文本的社交网络，可能是 Twitter 的竞争对手，代号 P92。你能对这些传言说些什么吗？

turns.en: There is a project. You know, I've always thought that sort of a text-based kind of information utility is just a really important thing to society. And for whatever reason, I feel like Twitter has not lived up to what I would have thought its full potential should be. And I think that the current... I think Elon thinks that right, and that's probably one of the reasons why you bought it. And I do think that there are ways to consider alternative approaches to this. And one that I think is potentially interesting is this open and federated approach where you're seeing with Mastodon and you're seeing that a little bit with BlueSky. And I think that it's possible that something that melds some of those ideas with the graph and identity system that people have already cultivated on Instagram could be a kind of very welcome contribution to that space. But I know we work on a lot of things all the time though too, so I don't want to get ahead of myself. We have projects that explore a lot of different things, and this is certainly one that I think could be interesting.
turns.zh: 确实有一个项目。你知道，我一直认为那种基于文本的信息工具对社会来说非常重要。而且不知为何，我觉得 Twitter 没有达到我认为它应有的全部潜力。我认为现在的……我觉得 Elon 也这么认为，这可能是他收购它的原因之一。我确实认为有办法考虑替代方案。其中一个我觉得可能有趣的是这种开放和联邦化的方法，你在 Mastodon 上看到了，在 BlueSky 上也看到了一点。我认为有可能将其中一些想法与人们已经在 Instagram 上建立的图谱和身份系统融合起来，这可能对这个领域是一个非常受欢迎的贡献。但我知道我们一直在做很多事情，所以我不想操之过急。我们有探索很多不同事物的项目，这当然是一个我认为可能有趣的项目。

turns.en: So what's the release launch date of that again? Or what's the official website?
turns.zh: 那么，那个的发布启动日期是什么？或者官方网站是什么？

turns.en: Well, we don't have that yet. Okay, but I... All right. And look, I mean, I don't know exactly how this is going to turn out. I mean, what I can say is yeah, there's some people working on this right. I think that there's something there that is interesting to explore.
turns.zh: 嗯，我们还没有那个。好吧，但我……好的。而且你看，我的意思是，我不确切知道这会如何发展。我的意思是，我能说的是，是的，有一些人在做这个。我认为那里有一些值得探索的有趣东西。

turns.en: If you look at it, it'd be interesting to just ask this question and throw Twitter into the mix. At the landscape of social networks that is Facebook, that is Instagram, that is WhatsApp, and then think of a text-based social network. When you look at that landscape, what are the interesting differences to you? Why do we have these different flavors? What are the needs, what are the use cases, what are the products? What is the aspect of...
turns.zh: 如果你看看这个，问这个问题并把 Twitter 放进去会很有趣。在 Facebook、Instagram、WhatsApp 的社交网络格局中，然后考虑一个基于文本的社交网络。当你看到这个格局时，对你来说有哪些有趣的差异？为什么我们有这些不同的风格？需求是什么，用例是什么，产品是什么？哪个方面……

ts.sec: Text-based social networks and their potential

turns.en: Them that create a fulfilling human experience and a connection between humans that is somehow distinct.
turns.zh: 它们创造了充实的人类体验，以及某种独特的人类之间的联系。

turns.en: Well, I think text is very accessible for people to transmit ideas and have back-and-forth exchanges. So it ends up being a good format for discussion in a lot of ways, uniquely good. If you look at other formats or networks focused on one type of content, like TikTok is obviously huge, and there are comments on TikTok, but the architecture is clearly that the video is primary and comments are afterthought. But one unique piece of having text-based content is that the comments can also be first-class. That makes it so conversations can filter and fork into all these different directions, which can be super useful. So there are a lot of things that are really awesome about the experience. It always struck me that Twitter should have a billion people using it, or whatever ends up being in that space. For whatever combination of reasons, these companies are complex organisms, and it's very hard to diagnose from the outside why a text-based, comment-as-first-citizen social network doesn't have a billion users.
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为文字非常便于人们传递思想和进行来回交流。所以它在很多方面都是一种很好的讨论形式，独特地好。如果你看看其他专注于单一内容类型的格式或网络，比如 TikTok 显然很庞大，上面也有评论，但架构很明显是视频为主，评论是附属品。但基于文字的内容有一个独特之处，就是评论也可以是一等公民。这使得对话可以过滤并分叉到各种不同的方向，这非常有用。所以这个体验有很多很棒的地方。我一直觉得 Twitter 应该有十亿用户，或者任何最终占据这个空间的东西。无论出于什么原因，这些公司都是复杂的有机体，从外部很难诊断为什么一个以文字评论为一等公民的社交网络没有十亿用户。

turns.en: Why doesn't Twitter, why doesn't a text-based comment-as-first-citizen social network have a billion users?
turns.zh: 为什么 Twitter，为什么一个以文字评论为一等公民的社交网络没有十亿用户？

turns.en: Well, I just think it's hard to build these companies. It's not that every idea automatically gets a billion people. It's just that that idea coupled with good execution should get there. But look, we hit certain thresholds over time. We kind of plateaued early on, and it wasn't clear we were ever going to reach 100 million people on Facebook. Then we got really good at dialing in internationalization and helping the service grow in different countries. That was a whole competence we needed to develop. Helping people spread the service to their friends was one of the things that, once we got very good at it, made me feel like if Instagram joined us early on, we could help grow that quickly, and same with WhatsApp. I think that's been a core competence we've developed and been able to execute on, and others have too. ByteDance obviously has done a very good job with TikTok and reached more than a billion people there. But it's certainly not automatic. You need a certain level of execution to get there. For whatever reason, Twitter has this great idea and magic in the service, but they just haven't cracked that piece yet. That's why you're seeing all these other things, whether it's Mastodon or BlueSky, that are maybe different cuts at the same thing.
turns.zh: 嗯，我只是觉得建立这些公司很难。并不是每个想法都能自动获得十亿用户。只是这个想法加上好的执行应该能达到。但你看，我们随着时间的推移达到了某些门槛。早期我们遇到了平台期，不清楚 Facebook 是否能达到一亿用户。然后我们非常擅长国际化，帮助服务在不同国家增长。那是我们需要培养的一整套能力。帮助人们把服务传播给朋友，一旦我们非常擅长这一点，就让我觉得如果 Instagram 早期加入我们，我们可以帮助它快速增长，WhatsApp 也一样。我认为这已经成为我们培养并能够执行的核心能力，其他人也有。字节跳动显然在 TikTok 上做得很好，在那里达到了超过十亿用户。但这当然不是自动的。你需要一定水平的执行才能达到。不管出于什么原因，Twitter 有这个好主意和服务的魔力，但他们就是还没有破解那部分。这就是为什么你看到所有这些其他东西，无论是 Mastodon 还是 BlueSky，可能都是对同一事物的不同尝试。

turns.en: I think through the last generation of social media overall, one of the interesting experiments that should get run at larger scale is what happens if there's somewhat more decentralized control and if the stack is more open throughout. I've been pretty fascinated by that and seeing how that works. To some degree, end-to-end encryption on WhatsApp, and as we bring it to other services, provides an element of that because it pushes the service really out to the edges. The server part we run for WhatsApp is relatively very thin compared to what we do on Facebook or Instagram, and much more of the complexity is in how the apps negotiate with each other to pass information in a fully end-to-end encrypted way. But I don't know, I think that's a good model. It puts more power in individuals' hands, and there are a lot of benefits if you can make it happen. Again, this is all pretty speculative. It's hard from the outside to know why anything does or doesn't work until you take a run at it. So I think it's an interesting thing to experiment with, but I don't really know where this one is going to go.
turns.zh: 我认为在上一代社交媒体的整体中，一个应该在更大规模上运行的有趣实验是，如果控制权更加去中心化，并且整个技术栈更加开放，会发生什么。我一直对此非常着迷，想看看它是如何运作的。在某种程度上，WhatsApp 上的端到端加密，以及我们将其引入其他服务，提供了这种元素，因为它将服务真正推向边缘。我们为 WhatsApp 运行的服务器部分相对非常精简，与我们在 Facebook 或 Instagram 上做的相比，更多的复杂性在于应用之间如何协商以完全端到端加密的方式传递信息。但我不确定，我认为这是一个好模型。它将更多权力交到个人手中，如果你能实现它，会有很多好处。再说一次，这都相当推测。从外部很难知道任何事情为什么有效或无效，直到你亲自尝试。所以我认为这是一个有趣的实验，但我真的不知道它会走向何方。

ts.sec: Elon Musk's Twitter acquisition and changes

turns.en: Since we were talking about Twitter, Elon Musk had what I think a few harsh words that I wish he didn't say. So let me ask, in the name of camaraderie, what do you think Elon is doing well with Twitter? And as a person who has run social networks like Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp for a long time, what can he do better, what can he improve on that text-based social network?
turns.zh: 既然我们刚才在聊 Twitter，埃隆·马斯克说了一些我认为有点刺耳的话，我希望他没说过。那么让我问问，本着友谊的精神，你认为埃隆在 Twitter 上做得好的是什么？作为一个长期运营 Facebook、Instagram、WhatsApp 等社交网络的人，他在那个基于文字的社交网络上可以做得更好、改进什么？

turns.en: Gosh, it's always very difficult to offer specific critiques from the outside before you get into this. I think one thing I've learned is that everyone has opinions on what you should do, and running the company you see a lot of specific nuances on things that are not apparent externally. I often think that some of the discourse around us could be better if there was more space for acknowledging that there are certain things we see internally that guide what we're doing. But since you asked, what is going well? I do think that Elon led a push early on to make Twitter a lot leaner. You can agree or disagree with all the tactics and how he did it. Obviously, every leader has their own style for how to execute dramatic changes. But a lot of the specific principles he pushed on, around basically trying to make the organization more technical, around decreasing the distance between engineers and him, fewer layers of management, I think those were generally good changes. I also think it was probably good for the industry that he made those changes, because my sense is that there were a lot of other people who thought those were good changes but who may have been a little shy about doing them. In my conversations with other founders and how people have reacted to the things we've done, what I've heard from a lot of folks is that when someone like you—when I outlined the organizational changes I wanted to make back in March—and when people see what Elon is doing, it gives people the ability to think through how to shape their organizations in a way that hopefully can be good for the industry and make all these companies more efficient.
turns.zh: 天哪，从外部给出具体的批评总是非常困难，在你真正进入之前。我认为我学到的一件事是，每个人对你应该做什么都有意见，而经营公司时你会看到很多外部不明显的具体细微差别。我经常认为，如果我们有更多空间承认内部有一些指导我们行动的事情，围绕我们的讨论可能会更好。但既然你问了，什么做得好？我确实认为埃隆早期推动让 Twitter 变得更精简。你可以同意或不同意所有的策略和做法。显然，每个领导者都有自己的风格来执行重大变革。但他推动的许多具体原则，比如让组织更技术化，减少工程师与他之间的距离，减少管理层级，我认为这些总体上是好的改变。我也认为他对行业做出这些改变可能是好事，因为我的感觉是，有很多其他人认为这些是好的改变，但可能有点不好意思去做。在我与其他创始人的对话以及人们对我们所做事情的反应中，我从很多人那里听到的是，当像你这样的人——当我在三月份概述我想要做的组织变革时——当人们看到埃隆在做什么时，它给了人们思考如何塑造自己组织的能力，希望这对行业有好处，让所有这些公司更高效。

turns.en: Productive over time, so something that was one where I think he was quite ahead of a bunch of the other companies on, and you know what he was doing there. Again, from the outside, very hard to know: did he cut too much? Did he not cut enough? Whatever. I don't think it's my place to opine on that. And you asked for a positive framing of the question: what do I admire? What do I think went well? But I think that certainly his actions led me and I think a lot of other folks in the industry to think about: hey, are we doing this as much as we should? Can we make our companies better by pushing on some of these same principles?
turns.zh: 随着时间的推移，效率越来越高。我认为他在这一点上领先于许多其他公司，而且你知道他在做什么。同样，从外部很难判断：他削减得太多还是不够？不管怎样，我觉得这不是我应该发表意见的地方。你问了一个积极角度的问题：我钦佩什么？我认为哪些方面做得好？但我认为他的行动确实让我，以及行业中的许多其他人开始思考：我们是否在应该做的程度上做了这些？我们能否通过推行一些同样的原则来让公司变得更好？

turns.en: Well, the two of you are at the top of the world in terms of leading the development of tech, and I wish there was more both-way camaraderie and kindness, more love in the world, because love is the answer. But let me ask on a point of efficiency: you recently announced multiple stages of layoffs at Meta. What are the most painful aspects of this process, given the painful effects it has on those people's lives?
turns.zh: 嗯，你们两位在引领技术发展方面处于世界之巅，我希望世界上有更多的双向情谊和善意，更多的爱，因为爱是答案。但让我问一个关于效率的问题：你最近在 Meta 宣布了多轮裁员。考虑到这对那些人生活的痛苦影响，这个过程中最痛苦的方面是什么？

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, that's it. And you basically have a significant number of people who, you know, this is just not the end of their time at Meta that they would have hoped for when they joined the company. Running a company, people are constantly joining and leaving for different reasons. But layoffs are uniquely challenging and tough in that you have a lot of people leaving for reasons not connected to their own performance or the culture not being a fit. It's really a kind of strategy decision and sometimes financially required. But not fully in our case, especially on the changes we made this year. A lot of it was more culturally and strategically driven by this push where I wanted us to become a stronger technology company with more of a focus on building more technical and higher quality products faster. I just view the external world as quite volatile right now, and I wanted to make sure that we had a stable position to be able to continue investing in these long-term ambitious projects around pushing AI forward and continuing to push forward all the metaverse work. In order to do that, in light of the pretty big thrash we had seen over the last 18 months — some of it macroeconomic, some competitively induced, some just because of bad decisions or things we got wrong — I decided that we needed to get to a point where we were a lot leaner. But then it's one thing to decide that at a high level; the question is how do you execute that as compassionately as possible. There's no good way, no perfect way for sure, and it's going to be tough no matter what. As a leader and team here, we've certainly spent a lot of time thinking: given that this is a thing that sucks, what is the most compassionate way we can do this? And that's what we've tried to do.
turns.zh: 是的，就是这样。你基本上有一大批人，他们加入公司时并没有预料到在 Meta 的时光会这样结束。经营一家公司，人们会因各种原因不断加入和离开。但裁员特别具有挑战性和艰难，因为很多人离开的原因与他们的个人表现或文化契合度无关。这实际上是一种战略决策，有时是财务上的需要。但在我们的案例中，尤其是今年所做的调整，并不完全是财务驱动的。很多是文化和战略上的推动，我希望我们成为一家更强大的科技公司，更专注于更快地构建更技术化、更高质量的产品。我认为外部世界现在相当动荡，我想确保我们有一个稳定的位置，能够继续投资于这些长期雄心勃勃的项目，比如推动 AI 向前发展，以及继续推进所有元宇宙工作。为了做到这一点，鉴于过去 18 个月我们经历的剧烈波动——有些是宏观经济引起的，有些是竞争引起的，有些只是因为错误的决策或我们搞砸的事情——我决定我们需要变得更加精简。但高层做出决定是一回事；问题是如何尽可能富有同情心地执行。没有好的方法，当然也没有完美的方法，无论如何都会很艰难。作为领导者和团队，我们确实花了很多时间思考：既然这是一件糟糕的事情，我们怎样才能以最富有同情心的方式去做？这就是我们努力去做的。

turns.en: You mentioned there's an increased focus on engineering, on tech, so technology teams, tech-focused teams, on building products.
turns.zh: 你提到更加关注工程和技术，也就是技术团队、以技术为中心的团队，专注于构建产品。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, I wanted to empower engineers more — the people who are building things, the technical teams. Part of that is making sure that the people who are building things aren't just at the leaf nodes of the organization. I don't want eight levels of management and then the people actually doing the work. So we made changes to make it that individual contributor engineers report at almost every level up the stack, which I think is important. Because when you're running a company, one of the big questions is latency of information. We talked about this a bit earlier in terms of the joy of the feedback you get doing something like Jiu-Jitsu compared to running a long-term project. But I actually think part of the art of running a company is trying to constantly re-engineer it so that your feedback loops get shorter so you can learn faster. Part of the way you do that is by — I kind of think that every layer you have in the organization means that information might need to get reviewed before it goes to you. Making it that the people doing the work are as close as possible to you is pretty important. So there's that. And I think over time, companies just build up very large support functions that are not doing the core technical work. Those functions are very important, but having them in the right proportion is important. If you try to do good work but don't have the right marketing team or legal advice, you're going to make some pretty big blunders. But at the same time, if you have too big of some of these support roles, then things might move a lot slower, maybe you're too conservative, or you move a lot slower than you should. Those are just examples. But how do you find that balance? That's really tough.
turns.zh: 是的，我的意思是，我想更多地赋能工程师——那些构建产品的人，技术团队。其中一部分是确保构建产品的人不仅仅处于组织的末端节点。我不希望有八层管理层，然后才是实际做工作的人。所以我们做了改变，让个人贡献者工程师在几乎每一层都有汇报关系，我认为这很重要。因为当你经营一家公司时，一个关键问题是信息延迟。我们之前谈到过，做柔术这样的活动得到的即时反馈与运营长期项目相比的乐趣。但我实际上认为，经营公司的一门艺术是不断重新设计它，使反馈循环更短，从而学得更快。做到这一点的方法之一是——我认为组织中的每一层都意味着信息在到达你之前可能需要经过审查。让做工作的人尽可能接近你是非常重要的。所以这是其一。而且我认为随着时间的推移，公司会建立起非常庞大的支持职能部门，它们不从事核心技术工作。这些职能非常重要，但保持适当的比例也很重要。如果你试图做好工作，但没有合适的营销团队或法律建议，你会犯一些相当大的错误。但与此同时，如果这些支持角色过于庞大，那么事情可能会变得非常缓慢，也许你会过于保守，或者比你本应有的速度慢得多。这些只是例子。但如何找到那个平衡？这真的很难。

turns.en: Yeah, no, but it's a constant equilibrium that you're searching for. How many managers to have? What are the pros and cons of managers?
turns.zh: 是的，没错，但这是一个你不断寻找的平衡。应该有多少管理者？管理者的利弊是什么？

turns.en: Well, I mean, I believe a lot in management. I think there are some people who think that it doesn't matter as much, but look, we have a lot of younger people at the company for whom this is their first job, and people need to grow and learn in their career. All that stuff is important. But here's one mathematical way to look at it. At the beginning of this, we asked our people team: what was the average number of reports that a manager had? I think it was around three, maybe three to four, but closer to three. I was like, wow, a manager can manage seven or eight people as best practice. But there was a reason why it was closer to three: it was because we were growing so quickly. When you're hiring so many people so quickly, you need managers who have capacity to onboard new people. Also, if you have a new manager, you may not want them to have seven direct reports immediately because you want them to ramp up. But going forward, I don't want us to...
turns.zh: 嗯，我的意思是，我非常相信管理。我认为有些人觉得管理没那么重要，但你看，我们公司有很多年轻人，这是他们的第一份工作，人们需要在职业生涯中成长和学习。所有这些都很重要。但这里有一个数学上的看法。在开始的时候，我们问了我们的人力团队：管理者平均有多少下属？我记得大约是三个，可能三到四个，但更接近三个。我想，哇，最佳实践是一个管理者可以管理七八个人。但为什么接近三个？那是因为我们增长得太快了。当你如此快速地招聘这么多人时，你需要有能力 onboarding 新人的管理者。另外，如果你有一个新管理者，你可能不希望他们立即有七个直接下属，因为你希望他们逐步适应。但展望未来，我不希望我们……

turns.en: Actually hire that many people that quickly, right? So I actually think we'll just do better work if we have more constraints and we're leaner as an organization. So in a world where we're not adding so many people as quickly, is it as valuable to have a lot of managers who have extra capacity waiting for new people? No, right. So now we can defragment the organization and get to a place where the average is closer to that seven or eight. And it just ends up being a somewhat more compact management structure, which decreases the latency on information going up and down the chain, and I think empowers people more. But I mean, that's an example that I think doesn't undervalue the importance of management and the personal growth or coaching that people need in order to do their jobs well. It's just, I think realistically, we're just not going to hire as many people going forward, so I think that you need a different structure.
turns.zh: 实际上，这么快就招那么多人，对吧？所以我其实认为，如果我们有更多约束、组织更精简，反而能做得更好。在一个不会那么快增加那么多人的世界里，养着一大堆等着新人的经理还有那么大的价值吗？没有，对吧。所以现在我们可以对组织进行去碎片化，让平均管理幅度接近七到八人。最终形成一个更紧凑的管理结构，这能减少信息在上下级之间传递的延迟，而且我认为也能更好地赋能员工。但我的意思是，这个例子并不是要贬低管理的重要性，也不是说人们做好工作就不需要个人成长或指导。只是，我认为现实是，未来我们不会招那么多人了，所以需要一种不同的结构。

turns.en: This whole incredible hierarchy and network of humans that make up a company is fascinating. Oh yeah, yeah. How do you hire great teams? How do you hire great—now with the focus on engineering and technical teams—how do you hire great engineers and great members of technical teams?
turns.zh: 构成公司的整个层级和人际网络真是令人着迷。哦，是的。你怎么招聘优秀的团队？现在重点是工程和技术团队，你怎么招聘优秀的工程师和技术团队成员？

turns.en: Well, you're asking how you select or how you attract them? Both. But select, I think attract is work on cool stuff and have a vision. I think that's right. And have a track record that people think you're actually going to be able to do it. Yeah. To me, the select seems like more of the art form, more of the tricky thing. Yeah. How do you select the people that fit the culture and can get integrated the most effectively, and so on? And maybe especially when they're young, to see the magic through the resume, through the paperwork and all this kind of stuff, to see that there's a special human there that would do incredible work.
turns.zh: 你问的是怎么筛选还是怎么吸引？两者都有。但筛选——我觉得吸引就是做酷的东西，有愿景。我觉得没错。还要有让人相信你确实能做到的过往记录。对我来说，筛选更像是一门艺术，更棘手。是啊。怎么筛选出那些契合文化、能最有效融入的人呢？尤其是年轻人，怎么透过简历、文件这些东西看到那种魔力，看到那里有一个能做出非凡成就的特别的人。

turns.en: So there are lots of different cuts on this question.
turns.zh: 所以这个问题有很多不同的角度。

turns.en: I mean, I think when an organization is growing quickly, one of the big questions that teams face is: do I hire this person who's in front of me now because they seem good, or do I hold out to get someone who's even better? And the heuristic that I always focused on for myself and my own direct hiring—that I think works when you recurse it through the organization—is that you should only hire someone to be on your team if you would be happy working for them in an alternate universe. Yeah. And that's basically how I've tried to build my team. It's, you know, I'm not in a rush to not be running the company, but I think in an alternate universe where one of these other folks was running the company, I'd be happy to work for them. I feel like I'd learn from them, I respect their general judgment, they're all very insightful, they have good values. And I think that gives you some rubric that you can apply at every layer. And I think if you apply that at every layer in the organization, then you'll have a pretty strong organization.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，当一个组织快速增长时，团队面临的一个大问题是：我是现在就雇这个看起来不错的人，还是再等等找一个更好的？我自己一直遵循的启发式方法——而且我认为当你在组织里层层推广时也有效——就是：你只应该雇那些在另一个宇宙里你愿意为他们工作的人。是的。这基本上就是我组建团队的方式。你知道，我并不急于不再管理公司，但我认为在另一个宇宙里，如果这些人中的某一位在管理公司，我会很乐意为他们工作。我觉得我能从他们身上学到东西，我尊重他们的总体判断，他们都非常有洞察力，价值观也很好。我认为这给了你一个可以在每一层应用的准则。而且我认为如果你在组织的每一层都应用这个准则，你就会有一个非常强大的组织。

turns.en: Okay. In an organization that's not growing as quickly, the questions might be a little different though. And there you asked about young people specifically, like people out of college. And one of the things that we see is—it's a pretty basic lesson—but like we have a much better sense of who the best people are who have interned at the company for a couple of months than by looking at them at a resume or a short interview loop. Obviously the in-person feel that you get from someone probably tells you more than the resume. And you can do some basic skills assessment, but a lot of the stuff really just is cultural. People thrive in different environments and on different teams, even within a specific company. And it's like the people who come for even a short period of time over a summer who do a great job here—you know that they're going to be great if they came and joined full-time. And that's one of the reasons why we've invested so much in internships—it's basically just a very useful sorting function, both for us and for the people who want to try out the company.
turns.zh: 好的。在一个增长不那么快的组织里，问题可能有点不同。你特别提到了年轻人，比如刚毕业的大学生。我们看到的一件事——这是一个很基本的经验——就是我们对那些在公司实习过几个月的人，比只看简历或短时间面试的人，要了解得多得多。显然，从一个人身上获得的面对面感觉可能比简历告诉你的更多。你可以做一些基本的技能评估，但很多东西真的只是文化问题。人们在不同的环境和团队中茁壮成长，即使是在同一家公司内部。那些来实习一个夏天、表现很好的人——你知道如果他们全职加入也会很出色。这就是为什么我们在实习上投入了这么多——它基本上是一个非常有用的筛选功能，对我们和对想尝试这家公司的人来说都是如此。

turns.en: You mentioned in person. What do you think about remote work? A topic that's been discussed extensively because of the pandemic over the past few years.
turns.zh: 你提到了面对面。你怎么看远程工作？这个话题因为过去几年的疫情被广泛讨论。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, I think it's a thing that's here to stay. But I think that there's value in both, right? It's not—I wouldn't want to run a fully remote company yet, at least. I think there's an asterisk on that, which is that some of the other stuff you're working on—yeah, yeah, exactly. It's like all the metaverse work and the ability to feel like you're truly present no matter where you are. I think once you have that all dialed in, then we may one day reach a point where it really just doesn't matter as much where you are physically. But I don't know, today it still does, right? So yeah, for people who have special skills and want to live in a place where we don't have an office, are we better off having them at the company? Absolutely. And a lot of people who work at the company for several years and build up the relationships internally, and have the trust and have a sense of how the company works—can they go work remotely now if they want and still do it as effectively? And we've done all these studies that show it's like, okay, does that affect their performance? It does not. But for the new folks who are joining, and for people who are earlier in their career and need to learn how to solve certain problems and need to get ramped up on the culture—when you're working through really complicated problems where you don't just want the formal meeting but you want to be able to brainstorm when you're walking in the hallway together after the meeting—I don't know, it's like we just haven't replaced the in-person dynamics there yet with anything remote. So yeah, there's a magic to the in-person that—we'll talk about this a little bit more, but I'm really excited by the possibility of the next few years in virtual reality and mixed reality that are possible with high resolution scans.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为它会一直存在。但我认为两者都有价值，对吧？我不会——至少现在，我不想运营一家完全远程的公司。我觉得这有个前提，那就是你正在做的其他一些事情——对，对，没错。就是所有元宇宙的工作，以及无论你在哪里都能感觉真正在场的能力。我认为一旦我们把那一切都搞定，那么也许有一天我们会达到一个点，物理位置真的不那么重要了。但我不确定，今天它仍然重要，对吧？所以，对于那些有特殊技能、想住在我们没有办公室的地方的人，我们让他们留在公司是不是更好？绝对是。还有很多在公司工作多年、建立了内部关系、有信任感、了解公司运作方式的人——他们现在如果想远程工作，还能同样高效吗？我们做了所有这些研究，结果显示：这会影响他们的绩效吗？不会。但对于新加入的人，以及那些职业生涯早期、需要学习如何解决某些问题、需要融入文化的人来说——当你处理非常复杂的问题时，你不仅想要正式的会议，还想在会后一起走过走廊时进行头脑风暴——我不知道，我们就是还没有用任何远程方式取代那种面对面的动态。所以，是的，面对面有一种魔力——我们稍后会多谈这个，但我对接下来几年通过高分辨率扫描实现的虚拟现实和混合现实的可能性感到非常兴奋。

turns.en: I, as a person who loves in-person interaction like these podcasts in person, it would be incredible to achieve the level of realism I've gotten a chance to witness. But let me ask about that. I got a chance to look at the Quest 3 headset and it is amazing. You've announced it, you'll get some more details in the fall, maybe release in the—when is it getting released again?
turns.zh: 作为一个喜欢面对面互动的人，比如这些播客的面对面录制，如果能达到我有机会见证的那种真实感，那将是不可思议的。但让我问问这个。我有机会看了 Quest 3 头显，它太棒了。你已经发布了它，秋天会有更多细节，也许发布在——它什么时候再次发布？

ts.sec: Quest 3 pricing and mixed reality features

turns.en: Forgot you mentioned it to me. We'll give more details of Connect, but it's coming this fall. Okay, so it's priced at $4.99. What features are you most excited about?
turns.zh: 你之前跟我提过，我们会在 Connect 上公布更多细节，不过它今年秋天就会上市。好，定价是 499 美元。你最兴奋的功能是什么？

turns.en: There are basically two big new things that we've added to Quest 3 over Quest 2. The first is high-resolution mixed reality. The basic idea is that you can think about virtual reality as having the headset and all the pixels are virtual, and you're basically immersed in a different world. Mixed reality is where you see the physical world around you and you can place virtual objects in it—whether that's a screen to watch a movie, a projection of your virtual desktop, or playing a game where zombies are coming out through the wall and you need to shoot them. Or playing Dungeons & Dragons or some board game and having a virtual version of the board in front of us while we're sitting here. All that is possible in mixed reality, and I think that is going to be the next big capability on top of virtual reality.
turns.zh: 我们在 Quest 3 上相比 Quest 2 主要增加了两大新功能。第一个是高分辨率混合现实。基本思路是：你可以把虚拟现实想象成戴上头显后所有像素都是虚拟的，你完全沉浸在一个不同的世界里。而混合现实则让你看到周围的物理世界，并能在其中放置虚拟物体——无论是看电影的屏幕、虚拟桌面的投影，还是玩一个僵尸从墙里钻出来需要你射击的游戏。或者玩龙与地下城之类的桌游，我们坐在这里，面前放一个虚拟的棋盘。所有这些在混合现实中都能实现，我认为这将是虚拟现实之上的下一个重大能力。

turns.en: It has done so well. I have to say, as a person who experienced it today with the zombies, having a full awareness of the environment and integrating that environment in the way they run at you while they try to kill you—it's just the mixed reality, the passthrough, is really, really well done. And the fact that it's only $500 is really well done. Thank you.
turns.zh: 它做得太好了。作为一个今天体验过僵尸模式的人，我必须说，完全感知环境并以那种方式融入环境——它们朝你跑来试图杀死你——混合现实的透视效果真的非常出色。而且它只要 500 美元，这真的很棒。谢谢。

turns.en: I mean, I'm super excited about it. We put a lot of work into making the device both as good as possible and as affordable as possible, because a big part of our mission and ethos is that we want people to be able to connect with each other. We want to reach and serve a lot of people. We want to bring this technology to everyone. So we're not just trying to serve an elite, wealthy crowd; we really want this to be accessible. That is, in a lot of ways, an extremely hard technical problem, because we don't have the ability to put an unlimited amount of hardware unless we need to basically deliver something that works really well but in an affordable package. We started with Quest Pro last year; it was $1,500, and now we've lowered the price to a thousand. But in a lot of ways, the mixed reality in Quest 3 is even better and more advanced than what we were able to deliver in Quest Pro. So I'm really proud of where we are with Quest 3 on that.
turns.zh: 我对此非常兴奋。我们投入了大量精力，力求让设备既尽可能好又尽可能实惠，因为我们使命和理念的核心是让人们能够彼此连接。我们希望触达并服务很多人，把这项技术带给每个人。所以我们不只是想服务精英富豪群体；我们真的希望它触手可及。这在很多方面都是一个极其困难的技术问题，因为我们不能无限制地堆硬件，而是要在实惠的价位上交付一款真正好用的产品。去年我们从 Quest Pro 起步，定价 1500 美元，现在降到了 1000 美元。但在很多方面，Quest 3 的混合现实比 Quest Pro 还要更好、更先进。所以我对 Quest 3 在这方面的表现非常自豪。

turns.en: It's going to work with all the virtual reality titles and everything that existed there, so people who want to play fully immersive games, social experiences, fitness—all that stuff will work. But now you'll also get mixed reality too, which I think people really like because sometimes you want to be super immersed in a game, but a lot of the time, especially when you're moving around, if you're active like doing some fitness experience—let's say you're doing boxing or something—you kind of want to be able to see the room around you so you know you're not going to punch a lamp or something like that.
turns.zh: 它将兼容所有已有的虚拟现实内容和游戏，所以想玩完全沉浸式游戏、社交体验、健身等的人都能用。但现在你还将获得混合现实功能，我觉得人们会非常喜欢，因为有时你想完全沉浸在游戏中，但很多时候，尤其是当你活动时——比如做健身体验，像打拳击之类的——你希望能看到周围的房间，这样你就不会打到台灯之类的东西。

turns.en: I don't know if you got to play with this experience, but we basically have this fun little demo we put together. It's like you're in a conference room or your living room, and you have the guy there and you're boxing him and fighting him, and all the other people are there too. I got a chance to do that. Yeah, and all the people are there—it's like that guy is right there in the room. And the other humans you're seeing, they can cheer you on, they can make fun of you if they're anything like friends of mine. It's really a compelling experience. I mean, VR is really interesting too, but this is something else—this becomes integrated into your life, into your world.
turns.zh: 我不知道你有没有玩过这个体验，但我们做了一个有趣的小演示。就像你在会议室或客厅里，有个人在那里，你和他打拳击，其他所有人也都在那里。我有机会试了一下。是的，所有人都在那里——那个人就在房间里。你看到的其他人可以为你加油，如果他们像我朋友那样，还会取笑你。这真的是一种引人入胜的体验。我是说，VR 也很有趣，但这完全是另一回事——它融入了你的生活，融入了你的世界。

turns.en: Yeah, and it's really a compelling experience. I think it's a completely new capability that will unlock a lot of different content. I think it'll also just make the experience more comfortable for a set of people who didn't want to have only fully immersive experiences. If you want experiences where you're grounded in your living room and the physical world around you, now you'll be able to have that too. I think that's pretty exciting.
turns.zh: 是的，这确实是一种引人入胜的体验。我认为这是一种全新的能力，将解锁大量不同的内容。它也会让那些不想只体验完全沉浸式的人感到更舒适。如果你想要那种扎根于客厅和周围物理世界的体验，现在你也能拥有了。我觉得这非常令人兴奋。

turns.en: I really liked how it added windows to a room with no windows. Yeah, me as a person—did you see the aquarium one where you could see the shark swim up? Or was that just the zombie one?
turns.zh: 我真的很喜欢它给没有窗户的房间加了窗户。是的，就我个人而言——你看到那个水族馆的体验了吗？能看到鲨鱼游过来？还是只有僵尸那个？

turns.en: The zombie one. But it's still—you don't necessarily want windows added to your living room where zombies come out, but in the context of that game, it's...
turns.zh: 是僵尸那个。不过你未必想在客厅里加一扇有僵尸钻出来的窗户，但在那个游戏的语境下，它……

turns.en: Yeah, yeah, yeah. I enjoyed it because you could see the nature outside, and as a person that doesn't have windows, it's just nice to have nature.
turns.zh: 对对对。我很喜欢，因为你能看到外面的自然景色，作为一个没有窗户的人，能看到自然真好。

turns.en: Yeah, well, even if it's a mixed reality setting, there's a Zen nature aspect to being able to look outside and alter your environment as you know it. There will probably be better, more Zen ways to do that than the zombie game you're describing, but you're right that the basic idea of having your physical environment on passthrough but then being able to bring in different elements externally—I think it's going to be super powerful.
turns.zh: 是的，即使在混合现实环境中，能够看向外面并改变你所知的环境，也有一种禅意的自然感。可能会有比你说的僵尸游戏更好、更禅意的方式来实现这一点，但你说得对，基本思路就是通过透视看到物理环境，同时能引入外部不同的元素——我认为这将非常强大。

turns.en: In some ways, I think mixed reality is also a predecessor to eventually getting AR glasses that are not the goggles form factor of the current generation of headsets. But I think a lot of the experiences that developers are making for mixed reality—basically putting a hologram in the world—will hopefully apply once we get the AR glasses too. Now that's got its own whole set of challenges.
turns.zh: 从某种意义上说，我认为混合现实也是最终实现 AR 眼镜的前身，那种眼镜不是当前头显的护目镜形态。但我认为开发者为混合现实打造的很多体验——基本上就是在世界中放置全息图——希望在我们推出 AR 眼镜时也能适用。当然，那本身也有一整套挑战。

turns.en: Well, the headset's already smaller than the previous version. Oh yeah, it's 40% thinner.
turns.zh: 嗯，头显已经比上一代小了。哦对，薄了 40%。

turns.en: And the other thing that I think is good about it—so mixed reality was the first big thing. The second is it's just a great VR headset. It's got 2x the graphics processing power, 40% sharper screens, 40% thinner, more comfortable, better strap architecture—all this stuff. If you liked Quest 2, I think this is just going to be all the content you might have played in Quest 2 getting sharper automatically and looking better in this. So I think people are really going to like it.
turns.zh: 另一个我觉得很好的方面——混合现实是第一个大亮点。第二个是它本身就是一款出色的 VR 头显。图形处理能力翻倍，屏幕清晰度提升 40%，薄了 40%，更舒适，头带结构也更好。如果你喜欢 Quest 2，那么你在 Quest 2 上玩过的所有内容在这上面都会自动变得更清晰、效果更好。所以我觉得人们会非常喜欢它。

turns.en: Yeah, so this fall. I have to ask: Apple just announced a mixed reality headset called Vision Pro for $3,500, available in early 2024. What do you think about this headset?
turns.zh: 嗯，今年秋天。我不得不问：苹果刚刚发布了一款名为 Vision Pro 的混合现实头显，售价 3499 美元，2024 年初上市。你怎么看这款头显？

turns.en: Well, I saw the materials when they launched. I...
turns.zh: 嗯，他们发布时我看了相关资料。我……

ts.sec: Apple Vision Pro and Quest 3 Comparison

turns.en: I haven't gotten a chance to play with it yet, so take everything with a grain of salt. But a few high-level thoughts. First, I do think this is a certain level of validation for the category. We were the primary folks out there before saying that virtual reality, augmented reality, mixed reality is going to be a big part of the next computing platform. Having Apple come in and share that vision will make a lot of people who are fans of their products really consider it. Then, of course, the $3,500 price. On one hand, I get it with all the stuff they're trying to pack in there. On the other hand, a lot of people aren't going to find that affordable. So I think there's a chance that them coming in actually increases demand for the overall space, and Quest 3 is the primary beneficiary. A lot of people might say, 'I like this, I'll give it another consideration,' or 'Now I understand what mixed reality is more,' and Quest 3 is the best one on the market that they can afford. It's great too. In our own way, we have a lot of features where we're leading. So I think that could be quite good. Over time, the companies are focused on somewhat different things. Apple has always focused on building really high-end things, whereas our focus is more democratic—we want to build things accessible to a wider number of people. We've sold tens of millions of Quest devices. My understanding, based on rumors (I don't have any special knowledge), is that Apple is building about 1 million of their device. So in terms of expected sales numbers, Quest is going to be the primary thing people in the market will continue using for the foreseeable future. Over the long term, it's up to the companies to see how well we execute. We come at it from different places. We're very focused on social interaction, communication, being more active—fitness, gaming, those things. Whereas a lot of the use cases in Apple's launch material were more around people sitting, looking at screens. Those are great—I think you will replace your laptop with a headset over time—but in terms of the different use cases the companies are going after, they're a bit different for where we are right now.
turns.zh: 我还没机会上手体验，所以先别全信。但有几个高层次的看法。首先，我认为这在一定程度上验证了这个品类。我们之前是主要说 VR、AR、混合现实将成为下一个计算平台重要组成部分的人。苹果加入并分享这一愿景，会让很多喜欢他们产品的人认真考虑。当然，还有 3500 美元的价格。一方面，我理解他们要塞进那么多东西；另一方面，很多人会觉得买不起。所以我认为苹果的加入反而可能增加整个市场的需求，而 Quest 3 会是主要受益者。很多人可能会说，‘我喜欢这个，我会再考虑一下’，或者‘现在我更了解混合现实了’，而 Quest 3 是市场上他们能买得起的最好的产品。它也很棒。我们有自己的方式，有很多领先的功能。所以我认为这可能会相当不错。长期来看，两家公司关注的重点有所不同。苹果一直专注于打造非常高端的设备，而我们的理念更民主——我们希望让更多人用得起。我们已经卖出了数千万台 Quest 设备。据我了解（基于传闻，我没有内幕消息），苹果计划生产约 100 万台他们的设备。所以从预期销量来看，Quest 在可预见的未来仍将是市场主流。长期来看，要看两家公司各自执行得如何。我们的出发点不同。我们非常关注社交互动、沟通、更活跃的体验——健身、游戏等等。而苹果发布材料中的很多用例更多是让人们坐着看屏幕。这些也很好——我认为随着时间的推移，你会用头显取代笔记本电脑——但就目前两家公司追求的不同用例而言，它们还是有些差异的。

turns.en: Yeah, so gaming wasn't a big part of the presentation, which is interesting. It feels like mixed reality gaming is such a big part of that; it was interesting to see it missing in the presentation.
turns.zh: 是的，所以游戏并不是演示的重点，这很有意思。感觉混合现实游戏应该是很重要的一部分，看到它缺席挺有趣的。

turns.en: Well, look, there are certain design trade-offs. They made this point about not wanting to have controllers. On one hand, there's a certain elegance about just being able to navigate the system with eye gaze and hand tracking. By the way, you'll be able to navigate Quest with your hands too if you want. One thing I should mention is that the capability from the camera with computer vision to detect certain aspects of the hand, allowing you to have a controller without that ring thing. The hand tracking in Quest 3 and the controller tracking is a big step up from the last generation. One of the demos we have is an MR experience teaching you how to play piano, where it highlights the notes you need to play, and it's all hands, no controllers. But if you care about gaming, having a controller allows you to have a more tactile feel and capture fine motor movement much more precisely than with hands without something to touch. So again, there are questions around what use cases you're optimizing for. If you want to play games, then you want to design the system in a different way. We're more focused on social experiences, entertainment experiences. Whereas if what you want is to make sure the text you read on a screen is as crisp as possible, then you need to make the design and cost trade-offs that lead to a $3,500 device. So I think there is a use case for that for sure, but the companies have basically made different design trade-offs to serve the use cases we're trying to target.
turns.zh: 嗯，你看，这里有一些设计上的取舍。他们强调不想用控制器。一方面，仅用眼球追踪和手势追踪就能导航系统，确实很优雅。顺便说一句，如果你愿意，也可以用双手来操作 Quest。有一点我该提一下：通过摄像头和计算机视觉检测手部某些特征的能力，让你可以拥有一个没有那个环的控制器。Quest 3 的手势追踪和控制器追踪相比上一代有了很大提升。我们有一个演示是混合现实体验，教你弹钢琴，它会高亮显示你需要弹的音符，全程用手，没有控制器。但如果你在乎游戏，有控制器能提供更触觉化的感受，并且比没有触摸物的手更精确地捕捉精细动作。所以，问题又回到了你为哪些用例做优化。如果你想玩游戏，那么你需要以不同的方式设计系统。我们更专注于社交体验、娱乐体验。而如果你想确保屏幕上阅读的文字尽可能清晰，那么你就需要做出设计和成本上的取舍，最终造出 3500 美元的设备。所以我认为这当然有它的用例，但两家公司基本上做了不同的设计取舍，以服务于我们各自瞄准的用例。

turns.en: There's a lot of other stuff I would love to talk to you about, about the metaverse, especially the Kodak Avatar, which I've gotten to experience a lot of different variations of recently. I'm really excited. I'll have to wait a little bit because I think there's a lot more to show off in that regard. But let me step back to AI. I think we've mentioned it a little bit, but I'd like to linger on this question that folks like Eliezer Yudkowsky have a worry about, and others, of the existential serious threats of AI that have been reinvigorated now with the rapid development of AI systems. Do you worry about the existential risks of AI as Eliezer does, about the alignment problem, about this getting out of hand anytime? There are a number of serious people who are raising a concern that is existential about something that you're involved with.
turns.zh: 还有很多其他东西我想和你聊，关于元宇宙，尤其是柯达头像，我最近体验了很多不同的版本。我非常兴奋。我可能得等一等，因为我觉得这方面还有很多可以展示的。但让我先回到 AI。我想我们之前提到了一点，但我想多谈谈这个问题，像 Eliezer Yudkowsky 和其他一些人担心的那样，随着 AI 系统的快速发展，存在严重的生存威胁。你是否像 Eliezer 那样担心 AI 的生存风险、对齐问题，以及它随时可能失控？有很多严肃的人对你所从事的事情提出了生存层面的担忧。

turns.en: I think you have to think about it, right? So I've spent quite a bit of time thinking about it from that perspective. The thing where I have basically come out on this for now is: I do think that over time, we need to think about this even more as we approach something that could be closer to superintelligence. I just think it's pretty clear to anyone working on these projects today that we're not there. One of my concerns is that we spent a fair amount of time on this before, but there are more—I don't know if 'mundane' is the right word—but there are concerns that already exist, like people using AI tools to do harmful things of the type we're already aware of: fraud, scams, or different things like that. And that's going to be a big issue.
turns.zh: 我认为你必须考虑这个问题，对吧？所以我花了不少时间从这个角度思考。目前我基本得出的结论是：我确实认为随着时间的推移，当我们接近可能更接近超级智能的东西时，我们需要更多地思考这个问题。我只是觉得，今天任何从事这些项目的人都很清楚，我们还没到那一步。我的一个担忧是，我们之前花了不少时间在这上面，但还有更多——我不知道‘平凡’这个词是否合适——但已经存在一些担忧，比如人们使用 AI 工具做有害的事情，我们已知的类型：欺诈、诈骗等等。这将会是一个大问题。

turns.en: There's a pretty big set of challenges that the company is working on that they're going to need to grapple with regardless of whether there is an existential concern at some point down the road. So I do worry that to some degree people can get a little too focused on some of the tail risk and then not do as good of a job as we need to on the things that you can be almost certain are going to come down the pipe as real risks that manifest themselves in the near term. For me, I've spent most of my time on that once I made the realization that the size of models we're talking about now in terms of what we're building are quite far from the superintelligence type concerns that people raise. But I think once we get a couple steps closer to that, as we do get closer, I think there are going to be some novel risks and issues about how we make sure the systems are safe for sure.
turns.zh: 公司正在应对一系列相当大的挑战，无论未来是否存在生存危机，这些挑战都必须解决。所以我确实担心，在某种程度上，人们可能过于关注一些尾部风险，而没有在我们几乎可以确定会出现的、在短期内显现的现实风险上做好必要的工作。对我来说，一旦我意识到我们现在正在构建的模型规模与人们提出的超级智能类担忧相去甚远，我就把大部分时间花在了这上面。但我认为，当我们再接近几步时，随着我们越来越接近，肯定会出现一些新的风险和问题，关于如何确保系统的安全。

turns.en: I guess here just to take the conversation in a somewhat different direction, I think in some of these debates around safety, the concepts of intelligence and autonomy or the being of the thing as an analogy get conflated together. And I think it very well could be the case that you can make something and scale intelligence quite far, but that may not manifest the safety concerns that people are saying. I mean, just if you look at human biology, we have our neocortexes where all the thinking happens, but it's not really calling the shots at the end of the day. We have a much more primitive old brain structure for which our neocortex, this powerful machinery, is basically just a prediction and reasoning engine to help our very simple brain decide how to plan and do what it needs to do in order to achieve these very basic impulses. And I think you can think about some of the development of intelligence along the same lines. Just like our neocortex doesn't have free will or autonomy, we might develop these wildly intelligent systems that are much more intelligent than our neocortex, have much more capacity, but are in the same way that our neocortex is sort of subservient and is used as a tool by our simple brain. I think it's not out of the question that very intelligent systems that have the capacity to think will act as sort of an extension of the neocortex doing that. So my own view is that where we really need to be careful is on the development of autonomy and how we think about that, because it's actually the case that relatively simple and unintelligent things that have runaway autonomy and just spread themselves — we have a word for that, it's a virus. It can be simple computer code that is not particularly intelligent but just spreads itself and does a lot of harm, biologically or computer. I just think that these are somewhat separable things, and a lot of what we need to develop when people talk about safety and responsibility is really the governance on the autonomy that can be given to systems. To me, if I were a policy maker or thinking about this, I would really want to think about that distinction between these, where building intelligence systems can create a huge advance in terms of people's quality of life and productivity growth in the economy, but it's the autonomy part of this that we really need to make progress on how to govern these things responsibly before we build the capacity for them to make a lot of decisions on their own or give them goals or things like that. That's a research problem, but I do think that to some degree these are somewhat separable things.
turns.zh: 我想把对话引向一个稍微不同的方向。我认为在一些关于安全的辩论中，智能和自主性——或者说事物的“存在”——这些概念被混为一谈了。很可能的情况是，你可以制造出某种东西，并将智能扩展到相当高的程度，但这可能并不会引发人们所说的那些安全问题。我的意思是，只要看看人类生物学：我们有新皮层，所有思考都在那里发生，但最终它并不是真正的决策者。我们有一个更原始的旧脑结构，而新皮层——这个强大的机器——基本上只是一个预测和推理引擎，帮助我们的简单大脑决定如何规划、如何行动，以实现这些非常基本的冲动。我认为你可以用同样的思路来思考智能的发展。就像我们的新皮层没有自由意志或自主性一样，我们可能会开发出这些极其智能的系统，它们比我们的新皮层聪明得多、能力更强，但就像新皮层从属于旧脑并被用作工具一样，这些系统也可能如此。我认为，拥有思考能力的非常智能的系统有可能充当新皮层的延伸。所以我的观点是，我们真正需要小心的是自主性的发展以及我们如何看待它，因为实际上，相对简单且不智能的事物如果拥有失控的自主性并自行传播——我们有一个词来形容它，那就是病毒。它可以是简单的计算机代码，并不特别智能，但只是自我传播并造成大量伤害，无论是生物上还是计算机上。我认为这些在某种程度上是可分离的。当人们谈论安全和责任时，我们需要发展的很多东西实际上是对系统可以获得的自主性的治理。对我来说，如果我是政策制定者或思考这个问题，我真的会想区分这两者：构建智能系统可以在人们的生活质量和经济生产力增长方面带来巨大进步，但自主性这部分才是我们真正需要取得进展的地方——在构建系统自主做出大量决策或赋予它们目标的能力之前，如何负责任地治理这些东西。这是一个研究问题，但我确实认为在某种程度上这些是可分离的。

turns.en: I love the distinction between intelligence and autonomy and the metaphor within neocortex. Let me ask about power. So building superintelligence systems, even if it's not in the near term, I think Meta is one of the few companies, if not the main company, that will develop the superintelligence system. And you are a man who's at the head of this company. Building AGI might make you the most powerful man in the world. Do you worry that that power will corrupt you?
turns.zh: 我喜欢你对智能和自主性的区分，以及新皮层这个比喻。让我问问关于权力的问题。构建超级智能系统，即使不是在短期内，我认为 Meta 是少数几家——如果不是主要的话——会开发超级智能系统的公司之一。而你正是这家公司的掌舵人。构建 AGI 可能会让你成为世界上最有权势的人。你担心这种权力会腐蚀你吗？

turns.en: What a question. I mean, look, I think realistically this gets back to the open source things that we talked about before, which is I don't think that the world will be best served by any small number of organizations having this without it being something that is more broadly available. And I think if you look through history, when there are these sort of unipolar advances and power imbalances, they tend to lead to kind of weird situations. So this is one of the reasons why I think open source is generally the right approach. And I think it's a categorically different question today when we're not close to superintelligence. I think there's a good chance that even once we get closer to superintelligence, open sourcing remains the right approach, even though I think at that point it's a somewhat different debate. But I think part of that is that it's one of the best ways to ensure that the system is as secure and safe as possible, because it's not just about a lot of people having access to it; it's the scrutiny that comes with building an open source system. This is a pretty widely accepted thing about open source: you have the code out there, so anyone can see the vulnerabilities, anyone can mess with it in different ways, people can spin off their own projects and experiment in a ton of different ways, and the net result of all of that is that the system gets hardened and becomes a lot safer and more secure. So I think there's a chance that this ends up being the way that this goes too, a pretty good chance, and that having this be open both leads to a healthier development of the technology and also leads to a more balanced distribution of the technology in a way that strikes good values to aspire to.
turns.zh: 好问题。我的意思是，实际上这又回到了我们之前讨论过的开源问题。我认为，如果只有少数组织拥有这项技术而不广泛开放，这对世界并不是最好的。如果你回顾历史，当出现这种单极进步和权力不平衡时，往往会导致一些奇怪的局面。所以这是我认为开源通常是正确方法的原因之一。而且我认为，在我们尚未接近超级智能的今天，这是一个截然不同的问题。我认为即使我们更接近超级智能，开源仍然很可能是正确的方法，尽管到那时讨论会有所不同。但部分原因在于，这是确保系统尽可能安全可靠的最佳方式之一，因为这不仅仅是让很多人能够访问它，而是开源系统带来的审查。关于开源有一个广为接受的观点：代码公开，任何人都可以看到漏洞，任何人都可以用不同方式摆弄它，人们可以衍生出自己的项目并进行大量实验，所有这些的最终结果是系统得到加固，变得更加安全可靠。所以我认为这很可能会成为最终的方向，而且开源既能带来更健康的技术发展，也能带来更平衡的技术分布，这符合值得追求的良好价值观。

turns.en: So to you, the risks of open sourcing are there, but the benefits outweigh the risks. And it's interesting, I think the way you put it, you put it well, that there's a different discussion now than when we get closer to the development of superintelligence, of the benefits and risks of open sourcing.
turns.zh: 所以对你来说，开源的风险是存在的，但收益大于风险。而且很有趣，我认为你表述得很好，现在关于开源收益和风险的讨论，与我们更接近超级智能开发时的讨论是不同的。

turns.en: Yeah, to be clear, I feel quite confident in the assessment that open sourcing models now is net positive. I think there's a good argument that in the future it will be too, even as you get closer to superintelligence.
turns.zh: 是的，明确地说，我对目前开源模型是净正面的评估非常有信心。我认为有充分的理由表明，未来即使更接近超级智能，开源也仍然是净正面的。

turns.en: What year do you think we'll have a superintelligence?
turns.zh: 你觉得我们哪一年会拥有超级智能？

turns.en: I don't know. That's pure speculation. I think it's very clear, just taking a step back, that we had a big breakthrough in the last year, right? Where the LLMs and diffusion models basically reached a scale where they're able to do some pretty interesting things. Then the question is what happens from here. To paint the two extremes: on one side, it's like, okay, we just had one breakthrough. If we have another breakthrough like that, or maybe two, then we could have something truly crazy, just so much more advanced. On that side, maybe we're only a couple of big steps away from reaching something that looks more like general intelligence. The other side, which we've historically seen a lot more, is that a breakthrough leads to the Gartner hype cycle: there's the hype, then the trough of disillusionment. People think, 'Hey, big breakthrough, maybe we're about to get another,' but actually you're not. You might have to sit with this one for a while. It could be 5 years, 10 years, 15 years until you figure out the next big thing. The fact that we just had this breakthrough puts us at a point of very wide error bars on what happens next.
turns.zh: 我不知道，这纯粹是猜测。我认为很明显，退一步看，去年我们取得了一个重大突破，对吧？LLM 和扩散模型基本上达到了一个规模，能够做一些相当有趣的事情。接下来的问题是，从这里会发生什么。描绘两个极端：一方面，我们刚刚取得了一个突破。如果再有一个类似的突破，或者两个，那么我们就可能拥有真正疯狂的东西，先进得多。在那个观点下，我们可能只需要几个大步就能接近看起来更像通用智能的东西。另一方面，历史上我们更常见的是，一个突破会引发 Gartner 炒作周期：先是炒作，然后是幻灭低谷。人们认为，“嘿，重大突破，也许我们即将迎来另一个”，但实际上并没有。你可能需要在这个突破上停留一段时间。可能是 5 年、10 年、15 年，直到你弄清楚下一个需要解决的大问题。我们刚刚取得这个突破的事实，使得接下来发生的事情的误差范围非常大。

turns.en: I think the traditional technical view, or looking at the industry, would suggest that we're not just going to stack breakthrough on top of breakthrough every six months. I would guess it will take somewhat longer in between these. But I tend to be pretty optimistic about breakthroughs too. So if you normalized for my normal optimism, then maybe it would be even slower than what I'm saying. But even within that, I'm not opining on how many breakthroughs are required to get to general intelligence because no one knows. But this particular breakthrough was such a small step that resulted in such a big leap in performance as experienced by human beings that it makes you think: as we stumble across this very open world of research, will we stumble across another thing that will have a giant leap in performance? Also, we don't know exactly at which stage it's really going to be impressive, because it feels like it's really encroaching on impressive levels of intelligence. You still didn't answer the question of what year we're going to have superintelligence. I'd like to hold you to that. No, I'm just kidding. But is there something you could say about the timeline as you think about the development of AGI and superintelligence systems?
turns.zh: 我认为传统的技术观点，或者看看行业，会表明我们不会每六个月就堆叠一个突破。我猜这些突破之间的间隔会更长一些。但我对突破也相当乐观。所以如果你根据我通常的乐观情绪来调整，那么可能比我说的还要慢。但即便如此，我并不是在评论需要多少突破才能达到通用智能，因为没人知道。但这次突破是如此小的一步，却导致了人类体验到的性能的巨大飞跃，这让你思考：当我们在这个非常开放的研究世界中摸索时，我们是否会偶然发现另一件能带来巨大性能飞跃的东西？而且，我们也不确切知道在哪个阶段它会真正令人印象深刻，因为它感觉确实正在逼近令人印象深刻的智能水平。你仍然没有回答我们将在哪一年拥有超级智能的问题。我想让你回答这个问题。不，我只是开玩笑。但关于你思考 AGI 和超级智能系统发展的时间线，你能说些什么吗？

turns.en: Sure. I still don't think I have any particular insight on when a singular AI system that is a general intelligence will get created. But I think that one thing that most people in the discourse that I've seen about this haven't really grappled with is that we do seem to have organizations and structures in the world that exhibit greater than human intelligence already. For example, a company acts as an entity with a singular brand, obviously a collection of people, but I certainly hope that Meta with tens of thousands of people makes smarter decisions than one person. That would be pretty bad if it didn't. Another example that is even more removed from the personification of intelligence is the stock market. It takes inputs, it's a distributed system, a cybernetic organism that millions of people around the world are basically voting on every day by choosing what to invest in. It's an organism or structure that is smarter than any individual, which we use to allocate capital as efficiently as possible around the world. I do think that these cyber systems that meld the intelligence of multiple people together, or meld the intelligence of multiple people and technology together, to form something dramatically more intelligent than any individual, seem to exist and seem to be harnessed productively for society as long as we build these structures in balance with each other. So that at least gives me hope. As we advance the technology, I don't know how long exactly it's going to be, but to some degree we already have many organizations in the world that are smarter than a single human, and that seems generally productive in advancing humanity.
turns.zh: 当然。我仍然认为我对何时会创造出单一的通用智能 AI 系统没有任何特别的见解。但我认为，在我看到的关于这个问题的讨论中，大多数人没有真正应对的一点是，我们似乎已经拥有展现出超越人类智能的组织和结构。例如，一家公司作为一个实体，拥有单一品牌，显然是一群人的集合，但我当然希望拥有数万人的 Meta 能比一个人做出更明智的决定。如果做不到，那就太糟糕了。另一个更远离智能人格化的例子是股票市场。它接收输入，是一个分布式系统，一个控制论有机体，全球数百万人每天通过选择投资什么来投票。它是一个比任何个体都更聪明的有机体或结构，我们用它来尽可能高效地在全球配置资本。我确实认为，这些将多人的智能融合在一起，或者将多人的智能与技术融合在一起，形成比任何个体都聪明得多的网络系统，似乎已经存在，并且只要我们以平衡的方式构建这些结构，就能为社会所利用。所以这至少给了我希望。随着我们推进技术，我不知道确切需要多长时间，但在某种程度上，世界上已经有许多组织比单个个体更聪明，这似乎总体上促进了人类进步。

turns.en: And somehow the individual AI systems empower the individual humans and the interaction between those humans to make that collective intelligence machinery that you're referring to smarter. So it's not like AI is becoming super intelligent; it's just becoming the engine that's making the collective intelligence that is primarily human more intelligent. It's educating the humans better, making them better informed, making it more efficient for them to communicate effectively and debate ideas. Through that process, the whole collective intelligence becomes more and more intelligent, maybe faster than the individual AI systems that are trained on human data anyway are becoming. Maybe the collective intelligence of the human species might outpace the development of AI.
turns.zh: 而且不知何故，单个 AI 系统赋能了个体人类以及这些人类之间的互动，使你提到的那个集体智能机器变得更聪明。所以并不是 AI 变得超级智能，而是它成为了让主要基于人类的集体智能更智能的引擎。它更好地教育人类，让他们更知情，让他们更有效地沟通和辩论想法。通过这个过程，整个集体智能变得越来越聪明，也许比那些基于人类数据训练的单个 AI 系统变得更快。也许人类物种的集体智能可能会超过 AI 的发展。

turns.en: I think there is a balance here. If a lot of the input that the systems are being trained on is basically coming from feedback from people, then a lot of the development does need to happen on human time. It's not like a machine will just be able to go learn all the stuff about how people think.
turns.zh: 我认为这里有一个平衡。如果系统训练所依赖的大量输入基本上来自人们的反馈，那么很多开发确实需要按照人类的时间来发生。机器不可能直接去学习所有关于人类如何思考的东西。

turns.en: There's a cycle to how this needs to work. This is an exciting world we're living in, and you're at the forefront of developing it. One of the ways you keep yourself humble, like we mentioned with jiu-jitsu, is doing some really difficult challenges, mental and physical. One you did very recently is the Murph Challenge, and you got a really good time. It's 100 pull-ups, 200 push-ups, 300 squats, and a mile run before and after. You got under 40 minutes on that. What was the hardest part? I think a lot of people were very impressed. It's a very impressive time. How crazy are you? That's the question I'm asking. But it wasn't your best time?
turns.zh: 这件事有一个循环的运作方式。我们生活在一个激动人心的世界，而你正处在发展的最前沿。你保持谦逊的方式之一，就像我们提到的柔术，是做一些非常困难的挑战，既有精神上的也有身体上的。你最近做的一个是“Murph 挑战”，而且你取得了非常好的成绩。它包括 100 个引体向上、200 个俯卧撑、300 个深蹲，以及前后各一英里跑。你在 40 分钟内完成了。最难的部分是什么？我想很多人都印象深刻，这确实是一个非常了不起的成绩。你有多疯狂？这就是我问的问题。但这不是你的最好成绩吧？

turns.en: I think anything under 40 minutes I'm happy with. It wasn't my best time, no. I think I've done it a little faster before, but not much. And of my friends, I did not win on Memorial Day. One of my friends did it actually several minutes faster than me. But just to clear up one thing that I saw a bunch of questions about on the internet: there are multiple ways to do the Murph Challenge. There's a kind of partitioned mode where you do sets of pull-ups, push-ups, and squats together, and then there's unpartitioned where you do the 100 pull-ups, then the 200 push-ups, then the 300 squats. Obviously, if you're doing them unpartitioned, it takes longer to get through the 100 pull-ups because anytime you're resting in between the pull-ups, you're not also doing push-ups and squats. So my unpartitioned time would be quite a bit slower. But no, I think at the end of the day, first of all, I think it's a good way to honor Memorial Day. It's Lieutenant Murphy basically, this was one of his favorite exercises, and I just try to do it on Memorial Day each year. It's a good workout. I got my older daughters to do it with me this time. My oldest daughter wants a weight vest because she sees me doing it with a weight vest. I don't know if a seven-year-old should be using a weight vest to do pull-ups. I was like, maybe I can make you a very lightweight vest, but I didn't think it was good for this. So she basically did a quarter Murph: she ran a quarter mile, then did 25 pull-ups, 50 push-ups, and 75 air squats, then ran another quarter mile in 15 minutes, which I was pretty impressed by. And my five-year-old too. So I was excited about that. I'm glad that I'm teaching them the value of physicality. I think a good day for my daughter is when she gets to go to the gym with me and cranks out a bunch of pull-ups. I love that about her. Hopefully I'm teaching her some good lessons.
